why: 'Advice' names a general kind of help or guidance, not separate countable 'advices', so English never adds 's' to it. If you want to refer to one instance, say 'a piece of advice', not 'an advice'.

wrong: I need some informations about the course.
right: I need some information about the course.
why: 'Information' describes a whole body of knowledge, not individual countable 'informations', so it stays singular with no 's'. Use 'a piece of information' or 'some information' when you want to be more specific.

wrong: He gave me many feedbacks on my work.
right: He gave me a lot of feedback on my work.
why: 'Feedback' is treated as an uncountable mass of comments and opinions, not separate countable items, so it doesn't take a plural form. Use 'a lot of feedback' or simply 'some feedback' to express quantity.

wrong: I have two luggages with me.
right: I have two pieces of luggage with me.
why: 'Luggage' refers to your bags and suitcases as one collective category, so it never becomes plural itself. To count individual bags, use 'a piece of luggage' or simply name the items (two bags, two suitcases).

wrong: She bought new furnitures for her home.
right: She bought new furniture for her home.
why: As with the earlier example, 'furniture' can never be made plural, no matter how many individual items you're describing. Practice saying 'some new furniture' instead of adding an 's'.

wrong: I need some equipments for the lab.
right: I need some equipment for the lab.
why: 'Equipment' describes tools and machines collectively as one category, so it stays singular even when referring to many items. Use 'a piece of equipment' if you want to talk about just one item.

wrong: He has a lot of knowledges about history.
right: He has a lot of knowledge about history.
why: 'Knowledge' represents an entire body of understanding, not separate countable 'knowledges', so it always stays singular. Simply say 'a lot of knowledge' or 'deep knowledge', with no 's' added.

wrong: I bought some breads from the store.
right: I bought some bread from the store.
why: 'Bread' refers to the food as a whole substance, not individual countable units, so it doesn't take a plural form (though 'loaves of bread' can describe distinct pieces). Just say 'some bread' to describe any quantity.

wrong: Can I have two waters, please?
right: Can I have two glasses of water, please?
why: 'Water' is an uncountable liquid, so on its own it never becomes plural - but you CAN count the containers holding it. Use 'glasses of water' or 'bottles of water' to specify amount.

wrong: She has beautiful hairs.
right: She has beautiful hair.
why: When talking about all the hair on someone's head as a whole, 'hair' is uncountable and stays singular (only use 'hairs' when counting individual strands, like 'three gray hairs'). For describing hair in general, always use the singular form.

wrong: I need some more moneys.
right: I need some more money.
why: 'Money' as a general concept is uncountable, so it never takes a plural 's', even though you can count individual coins or notes. Use 'some money' or 'a lot of money' to describe an amount.

wrong: There was a lot of traffics on the road.
right: There was a lot of traffic on the road.
why: 'Traffic' describes the overall flow of vehicles as one mass concept, not individual countable 'traffics'. Always keep 'traffic' singular, no matter how heavy it is.

wrong: I have too many homeworks today.
right: I have too much homework today.
why: 'Homework' is uncountable, so it pairs with 'much' (for uncountable nouns) rather than 'many' (for countable nouns), and it never takes an 's'. Remember this pairing rule: many + countable, much + uncountable.

wrong: She gave us useful informations.
right: She gave us useful information.
why: As before, 'information' never takes a plural form, regardless of how many separate facts it might contain. Keep practicing this word until 'informations' stops feeling natural to write.

wrong: I bought two soaps from the shop.
right: I bought two bars of soap from the shop.
why: 'Soap' as a substance is uncountable, so you count the containers or units it comes in (bars, bottles), not the soap itself. Say 'two bars of soap', not 'two soaps', unless referring to two different brands/types.

wrong: He gave me many advices about my career.
right: He gave me a lot of advice about my career.
why: This is the same rule as before applied again - 'advice' is always singular, no matter how many separate pieces of guidance were given. Repetition helps: practice 'a lot of advice' until it becomes automatic.

wrong: I need some new stationaries for school.
right: I need some new stationery for school.
why: 'Stationery' (pens, paper, and other writing supplies) is treated as one uncountable category in English, so it doesn't take a plural form. Use 'some stationery' or 'stationery items' to describe several different things.

wrong: There are many furnitures missing from the room.
right: There is a lot of furniture missing from the room.
why: Beyond just the noun, notice the verb changes too - since 'furniture' is singular (uncountable), the verb must be 'is', not 'are'. Uncountable nouns affect both the noun form and the verb that follows.

wrong: I have a work to finish today.
right: I have some work to finish today.
why: 'Work' meaning a task or job in general is uncountable, so it can't take 'a' directly in front of it (though 'a job' or 'a task' works fine as countable alternatives). Use 'some work' or simply 'work' with no article.

wrong: She has many experiences in teaching.
right: She has a lot of experience in teaching.
why: When 'experience' means general knowledge gained over time, it's uncountable (though 'experiences' can be countable when referring to specific separate events, like 'she shared some experiences from her trip'). For general skill or background, keep it singular: 'a lot of experience'.

wrong: I need an accommodation for tonight.
right: I need accommodation for tonight.
why: 'Accommodation' (a place to stay) is uncountable in British English, so it doesn't take 'a/an' or a plural form. Simply say 'I need accommodation' without any article.

wrong: He gave some good suggestions and advices.
right: He gave some good suggestions and advice.
why: Notice that 'suggestions' can correctly be plural (it's countable), but 'advice' next to it must stay singular, since the two words follow different rules. Always check each noun separately rather than assuming they behave the same way.

wrong: I have less problems than before.
right: I have fewer problems than before.
why: Although this is a countable/uncountable-related mistake, the fix is about word choice: use 'fewer' with countable nouns (problems) and 'less' with uncountable nouns (money, time). Remember: fewer + countable, less + uncountable.

wrong: There is less people at the party than expected.
right: There are fewer people at the party than expected.
why: 'People' is a countable plural noun, so it needs 'fewer' (not 'less') and the plural verb 'are'. This is one of the most common mistakes even among native speakers, so it's worth mastering carefully.

wrong: I don't have much friends here.
right: I don't have many friends here.
why: 'Friends' is countable, so it pairs with 'many', not 'much', which is reserved for uncountable nouns. Keep repeating the rule: many/few + countable, much/little + uncountable.

wrong: She doesn't have many money.
right: She doesn't have much money.
why: This is the reverse mistake - 'money' is uncountable, so it needs 'much', not 'many'. Practicing both directions (many friends / much money) together helps the rule stick.

wrong: I need a piece of advices.
right: I need a piece of advice.
why: Once you use 'a piece of' to count an uncountable noun, the noun itself must stay singular - 'a piece of advices' mixes singular and plural incorrectly. The phrase 'a piece of' already shows quantity, so the noun after it never needs its own 's'.

wrong: Can you give me two informations?
right: Can you give me two pieces of information?
why: Just like 'advice', if you want to count 'information', use 'pieces of information', not a number directly with the uncountable noun. Never place a number directly before an uncountable noun without a counting phrase like 'piece(s) of'.

wrong: I bought some new furnitures and clothes.
right: I bought some new furniture and clothes.
why: 'Furniture' stays singular (never 'furnitures'), while 'clothes' is a normal plural countable noun that's fine with an 's' - both fit naturally after 'some', but only one of them can take a plural ending. Check each noun in a sentence individually rather than assuming they all follow the same countable/uncountable rule.

wrong: I need one equipment for this experiment.
right: I need a piece of equipment for this experiment.
why: You can't place 'one' directly before an uncountable noun like 'equipment' - use 'a piece of equipment' instead to count a single item. This mirrors the same fix used for advice and information.

wrong: There were many thunders last night.
right: There was a lot of thunder last night.
why: 'Thunder' as a natural phenomenon is uncountable in English (you can talk about 'claps of thunder' to count individual sounds), so avoid making the word itself plural. Use 'a lot of thunder' to describe general amount.

wrong: The weather was full of rains today.
right: The weather was rainy today.
why: 'Rain' describing the weather in general is uncountable, so avoid pluralizing it - instead, consider using the adjective form 'rainy' to describe the day. This is a useful trick: sometimes switching to an adjective avoids the plural noun problem entirely.

wrong: I heard some good musics at the concert.
right: I heard some good music at the concert.
why: 'Music' as an art form is uncountable, so it never takes a plural 's', even when referring to many different songs. Use 'some music' or 'several songs' depending on what you actually mean.

wrong: She has a lot of homeworks this week.
right: She has a lot of homework this week.
why: Even with 'a lot of' (which works for both countable and uncountable nouns), 'homework' itself must remain singular. The quantity word changes ('many' vs 'a lot of'), but the uncountable noun's form never does.

wrong: I want to give you some good newses today.
right: I want to give you some good news today.
why: 'News' is uncountable and always singular in English, so it never takes a plural 's', no matter how many separate pieces of news there are. Remember this word specifically: news never becomes 'newses', in any context.

wrong: There are too much cars on this road.
right: There are too many cars on this road.
why: 'Cars' is countable, so it pairs with 'many', not 'much' - this is the same rule as friends/money, just reversed direction again. Whenever the noun is something you could count one by one, use 'many', never 'much'.

wrong: I don't have many time today.
right: I don't have much time today.
why: 'Time' (in the sense of duration) is uncountable, so it needs 'much', not 'many'. This mistake is extremely common because 'time' can sometimes be countable in other contexts (like 'three times'), which causes confusion.

wrong: She gave me two pieces of jewelries.
right: She gave me two pieces of jewelry.
why: 'Jewelry' is uncountable, so even after 'pieces of' (which already shows quantity), the word itself should stay singular, with no 's' added. Keep the noun in its base uncountable form even when counting phrases are used.

wrong: I want to buy some new furnitures for my office.
right: I want to buy some new furniture for my office.
why: This is the same core mistake repeated - 'furniture' simply never takes an 's', no matter the sentence around it. Repetition and practice with this specific word are the best way to make the correct form automatic.

wrong: There is too much people in this room.
right: There are too many people in this room.
why: As before, 'people' is countable plural, so use 'many' and the plural verb 'are', never 'much' and 'is'. This exact sentence pattern is worth memorizing as a fixed correct phrase.

wrong: I received an useful feedback.
right: I received useful feedback.
why: Since 'feedback' is uncountable, it can't take 'a/an' directly - simply drop the article and use the noun alone, or add 'some' before it. Whenever an uncountable noun feels like it needs 'a/an', that's a sign to just remove it.

wrong: I have less friends than her.
right: I have fewer friends than her.
why: This repeats the fewer/less rule - 'friends' is countable, so 'fewer' is correct, not 'less'. Keep testing yourself with this pair of words until it's second nature.

wrong: There was too much accidents this year.
right: There were too many accidents this year.
why: 'Accidents' is countable, so it needs 'many' and the plural verb 'were', not 'much' and 'was'. This mistake often happens because 'much' feels natural before certain nouns out of habit rather than logic.

wrong: She gave good advices to everyone.
right: She gave good advice to everyone.
why: This is the same 'advice' rule appearing once more - it's worth repeating because this is one of the single most common uncountable noun mistakes made by English learners worldwide. Keep 'advice' singular in every context, without exception.

wrong: I don't have much problems with this.
right: I don't have many problems with this.
why: 'Problems' is countable, so 'many' is correct, and 'much' would be wrong here. This is another version of the same recurring pattern - checking whether a noun is countable before choosing 'much' or 'many'.

wrong: He has few money left.
right: He has little money left.
why: Just as 'fewer/less' pairs with countable/uncountable, so do 'few/little' - use 'few' for countable nouns and 'little' for uncountable ones like money. Remember this second pair alongside fewer/less: few + countable, little + uncountable.

wrong: I only have little friends in this city.
right: I only have few friends in this city.
why: This is the reverse case - 'friends' is countable, so it needs 'few', not 'little'. Practicing both money/little and friends/few together will help you keep the pattern straight.

wrong: I need some informations before I decide.
right: I need some information before I decide.
why: This is a final repeat of the most common mistake in this whole list, included again because it's worth truly memorizing: 'information' never, ever takes an 's' in English, no matter the context.

wrong: We had a beautiful scenery on our trip.
right: We had beautiful scenery on our trip.
why: 'Scenery' is uncountable - it describes a whole view or landscape as one general idea, so it never takes 'a/an' in front of it. Simply describe it directly, without an article: 'beautiful scenery', not 'a beautiful scenery'.

wrong: She doesn't know nothing about this.
right: She doesn't know anything about this.
why: 'Doesn't' and 'nothing' are both negative, creating a double negative - pair 'doesn't' with 'anything' instead. A simple trick: after a negative verb (don't, doesn't, isn't), always switch to 'any-' words (anything, anyone, anywhere), not 'no-' words.

wrong: I can't hardly hear you.
right: I can hardly hear you.
why: 'Hardly' is already a negative-like word (meaning 'barely'), so adding 'can't' creates an unintended double negative that actually reverses the meaning. Remember: hardly, barely, and scarcely already carry negative meaning, so they should be used with a positive verb (can, will), not a negative one.

wrong: I don't have nobody to talk to.
right: I don't have anybody to talk to.
why: Just like 'nothing' and 'no', the word 'nobody' creates a double negative when combined with 'don't' - use 'anybody' instead. Keep applying the same rule: negative verb + 'any-' word, never 'no-' word.

wrong: I can't get no satisfaction.
right: I can't get any satisfaction.
why: This famous phrase (from a well-known song) is actually a double negative used deliberately for style - in standard, formal English, you should use 'any' after a negative verb, not 'no'. Save double negatives for song lyrics or informal dialects, and use single negatives in standard writing.

wrong: She isn't never on time.
right: She is never on time. / She isn't ever on time.
why: 'Isn't' and 'never' are both negative, so combining them is a double negative - choose only one negative word for the sentence. Either keep 'is' (positive) with 'never', or keep 'isn't' with 'ever' (not negative).

wrong: I don't want none of this.
right: I don't want any of this.
why: As with the other examples, 'don't' and 'none' together form a double negative - switch 'none' to 'any' after the negative verb. This is one of the most common double negative patterns, so it's worth practicing directly.

wrong: He didn't do nothing wrong.
right: He didn't do anything wrong.
why: This is one of the most frequent double negative mistakes - 'didn't' and 'nothing' cancel each other out logically (meaning he DID do something), even though the speaker meant the opposite. Always pair a negative verb with 'anything', never 'nothing', to avoid confusing the meaning.

wrong: I returned back to my hometown.
right: I returned to my hometown.
why: 'Return' already means 'to go back', so adding the word 'back' afterward repeats the same meaning unnecessarily - this is called redundancy. Simply say 'returned to', without the extra word 'back'.

wrong: Please repeat that again.
right: Please repeat that.
why: 'Repeat' already means 'to say again', so adding 'again' afterward is redundant, repeating the same idea twice. Just say 'repeat that', which already implies doing it again.

wrong: I need to plan ahead for the future.
right: I need to plan for the future. / I need to plan ahead.
why: 'Plan ahead' and 'for the future' express the same basic idea, so using both together is redundant - choose one or the other. Pick the phrase that fits your sentence better, but avoid stacking both.

wrong: She is a a young, little girl.
right: She is a young girl.
why: 'Young' and 'little' (when describing age) express overlapping meaning here, making the sentence repetitive - choose just one descriptive word, unless 'little' specifically refers to size, not age. Avoid piling up adjectives that all say the same thing.

wrong: The final outcome was surprising.
right: The outcome was surprising.
why: 'Outcome' already means the final result of something, so adding 'final' in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'the outcome', without 'final', unless you specifically need to distinguish it from earlier, temporary outcomes.

wrong: I saw it with my own eyes personally.
right: I saw it with my own eyes.
why: 'With my own eyes' already emphasizes personal, direct experience, so adding 'personally' afterward repeats the same emphasis unnecessarily. One strong phrase is enough - you don't need to stack emphasis words together.

wrong: The reason why I left is simple.
right: The reason I left is simple. / Why I left is simple.
why: 'The reason' and 'why' both introduce the same explanatory idea, so using them together repeats the function unnecessarily. Choose one: either 'the reason (that) I left' or 'why I left', not both combined.

wrong: It is completely full to the brim.
right: It is full to the brim. / It is completely full.
why: 'Full to the brim' already means completely full, so adding the word 'completely' beforehand repeats the same meaning. Pick the vivid phrase ('full to the brim') OR the simple one ('completely full'), not both stacked together.

wrong: I have two twin sisters.
right: I have twin sisters. / I have two sisters.
why: 'Twin' already implies there are exactly two (a pair), so adding the number 'two' beforehand repeats information already contained in the word 'twin'. Simply say 'twin sisters', letting the word 'twin' do the counting for you.

wrong: We need to collaborate together on this.
right: We need to collaborate on this.
why: 'Collaborate' already means to work together, so adding 'together' afterward repeats the same idea. The word 'collaborate' already contains the sense of joint effort, so no extra word is needed.

wrong: Please add an additional comment below.
right: Please add a comment below.
why: 'Add' already implies something extra being included, so pairing it with 'additional' repeats the same meaning. Simply say 'add a comment', without needing 'additional' as well.

wrong: This is an unexpected surprise.
right: This is a surprise. / This is unexpected.
why: 'Surprise' already implies something unexpected, so adding the word 'unexpected' beforehand repeats the same idea. Pick just one word to convey the meaning, since 'surprise' already carries the sense of being unforeseen.

wrong: I need to think about it in my mind.
right: I need to think about it.
why: 'Think' already happens in your mind, so adding 'in my mind' afterward states the obvious and repeats information. Simply say 'think about it', trusting that thinking is understood to happen mentally.

wrong: She nodded her head in agreement.
right: She nodded in agreement.
why: 'Nod' already refers to a movement of the head, so specifying 'her head' afterward repeats information already built into the verb. Just say 'nodded in agreement', without restating the body part.

wrong: He whispered quietly so no one could hear.
right: He whispered so no one could hear.
why: 'Whisper' already means to speak very quietly, so adding the word 'quietly' afterward repeats the same idea. Trust that 'whispered' alone communicates the soft volume clearly.

wrong: I am going to go there tomorrow.
right: I am going there tomorrow. / I will go there tomorrow.
why: Using both 'going to' and 'go' together creates unnecessary repetition of the same movement idea - choose either the 'going to' future form or the simple future with 'will', not a mix of both verb forms stacked together. Pick one future structure and stick with it in the sentence.

wrong: Each and every one of you matters.
right: Each of you matters. / Every one of you matters.
why: 'Each' and 'every' express very similar meanings, so combining them together, while common as an idiom for emphasis, is technically redundant in formal writing. This phrase is acceptable informally for emphasis, but for precise writing, choose just one word.

wrong: It was a total and complete disaster.
right: It was a total disaster. / It was a complete disaster.
why: 'Total' and 'complete' express the same idea of entirety, so using both together repeats the same emphasis unnecessarily. Pick one word - either works equally well on its own.

wrong: I revert back to you shortly.
right: I'll get back to you shortly. / I'll revert shortly.
why: 'Revert' already means to return or go back to a previous state, so adding 'back' afterward is redundant (this exact phrase is extremely common in business emails, but it's worth avoiding in careful writing). Use 'get back to you' or 'revert' alone, not 'revert back'.

wrong: Let's collaborate and work together on this project.
right: Let's collaborate on this project. / Let's work together on this project.
why: 'Collaborate' and 'work together' mean essentially the same thing, so using both phrases in one sentence repeats the same idea. Choose whichever phrase sounds more natural for your context, not both.

wrong: I need to add more additional details.
right: I need to add more details.
why: 'More' and 'additional' both mean extra amounts of something, so combining them together is repetitive. Just say 'add more details' or 'add additional details' - either alone communicates the same idea clearly.

wrong: This is my personal opinion.
right: This is my opinion. / In my opinion...
why: An 'opinion' is already personal by definition (it belongs to one person's view), so adding 'personal' beforehand is often unnecessary repetition (though it's very common in casual speech for extra emphasis). For cleaner writing, 'my opinion' alone is usually sufficient.

wrong: We collaborated jointly on this report.
right: We collaborated on this report.
why: 'Collaborate' already implies working jointly with others, so adding 'jointly' afterward is redundant. Trust that 'collaborate' fully expresses the idea of joint effort without needing reinforcement.

wrong: I close approximately around 500 sales last year.
right: I closed approximately 500 sales last year. / I closed around 500 sales last year.
why: 'Approximately' and 'around' both mean roughly or about, so pairing them together repeats the same idea of an estimate. Choose just one estimating word to keep the sentence precise and clean.

wrong: This meeting is optional and not compulsory.
right: This meeting is optional.
why: 'Optional' already means 'not required', so adding 'not compulsory' afterward simply repeats the opposite phrasing of the same idea. One clear word ('optional') is enough to communicate the meaning without restating it differently.

wrong: I need your final decision by tomorrow.
right: I need your decision by tomorrow.
why: 'Decision' often implies finality already, so 'final' can sometimes feel redundant, though this pairing is common and generally accepted in everyday English (unlike some other examples on this list, it's a milder case of possible redundancy). Consider dropping 'final' for tighter writing, but this one is a softer rule than the others.

wrong: I don't have anything nothing to say.
right: I don't have anything to say.
why: This sentence accidentally combines both the correct form (anything) and the incorrect double-negative form (nothing) together - remove the extra negative word entirely. Always proofread negative sentences carefully to make sure only one negative idea appears.

wrong: She can't never make up her mind.
right: She can never make up her mind. / She can't ever make up her mind.
why: 'Can't' and 'never' are both negative, creating a double negative - choose either the positive 'can' with 'never', or 'can't' with 'ever'. Both versions are grammatically correct and mean the same thing.

wrong: There wasn't nobody at the party.
right: There was nobody at the party. / There wasn't anybody at the party.
why: 'Wasn't' and 'nobody' together form a double negative - keep only one negative element in the sentence. This is the exact same pattern as 'isn't...nobody' covered earlier, just in the past tense.

wrong: I don't know nothing about cars.
right: I don't know anything about cars.
why: This repeats the classic double negative pattern once more, worth practicing until it's completely automatic: negative verb + 'any-' word, never 'no-' word. This exact type of sentence appears extremely often in casual speech, so it's worth correcting deliberately.

wrong: He wouldn't never agree to that.
right: He would never agree to that. / He wouldn't ever agree to that.
why: 'Wouldn't' and 'never' are both negative, so combining them breaks the single-negative rule. As always, pick one negative word for the sentence, either in the verb or in the adverb, not both.

wrong: I can't barely see anything in this fog.
right: I can barely see anything in this fog.
why: Just like 'hardly', the word 'barely' already carries a negative-like meaning, so pairing it with 'can't' creates an unintended double negative. Use 'barely' and 'hardly' with positive verbs (can, is, will), not negative ones.

wrong: Nobody doesn't like a good meal.
right: Nobody dislikes a good meal. / Everybody likes a good meal.
why: 'Nobody' and 'doesn't' are both negative, creating confusion about the actual meaning - restructure the sentence to use only one negative idea. Choose either a negative subject with a positive verb ('nobody likes'), or flip to a positive subject ('everybody likes').

wrong: The end result was exactly as we expected.
right: The result was exactly as we expected.
why: 'Result' already means the outcome or 'end' of a process, so adding 'end' beforehand repeats the same idea. Simply say 'the result', trusting that it already means the final outcome.

wrong: I am having two brothers.
right: I have two brothers.
why: 'Have' showing possession or family relationships is a state, not an action, so it should never be used in continuous ('-ing') form. This is one of the most common Indian English patterns - remember: possession and relationships use simple 'have/has', never 'am/is/are having'.

wrong: Do the needful.
right: Please take the necessary action. / Please handle this.
why: "Do the needful" is a phrase specific to Indian English that sounds outdated or unclear to international readers - it's better to state exactly what action you want taken. Replace vague, formal-sounding Indian English phrases with clear, specific requests in international business writing.

wrong: Revert back to me by tomorrow.
right: Please get back to me by tomorrow. / Please reply by tomorrow.
why: 'Revert' by itself already means 'to return to a previous state', so 'revert back' is redundant, and this phrase feels unclear to international readers expecting 'reply' or 'respond'. Replace 'revert back' with 'reply' or 'get back to' for clearer international communication.

wrong: I am not knowing the answer.
right: I don't know the answer.
why: 'Know' is a state verb and should never appear in continuous form - this is one of the most frequent patterns in Indian English influenced by regional language structures. Practice using simple present ('don't know') instead of the continuous form for all state verbs.

wrong: Kindly do one thing.
right: Could you please... / Please...
why: "Kindly do one thing" is a very common introductory phrase in Indian English, but it sounds unusual and overly formal to international readers - simply state your request directly with 'please' or 'could you'. Simplify introductory request phrases for clearer, more natural-sounding international English.

wrong: Myself Raj, I am working in IT.
right: My name is Raj, and I work in IT. / I am Raj, and I work in IT.
why: Starting an introduction with "Myself [name]" is common in Indian English but isn't standard grammatically - 'myself' is a reflexive pronoun, not meant to introduce yourself this way. Use 'My name is...' or 'I am...' as the standard way to introduce yourself in English.

wrong: What is your good name?
right: What is your name?
why: "Good name" is a direct translation from Hindi and other Indian languages, but in English, simply 'name' is used without 'good' in front. Ask simply 'What is your name?' - the word 'good' isn't needed or standard in English.

wrong: I passed out from college in 2015.
right: I graduated from college in 2015.
why: "Passed out" in international English actually means 'fainted' or 'lost consciousness' - the term for completing your studies is 'graduated'. Always use 'graduate/graduated' to describe completing an educational program, never 'passed out'.

wrong: Please open the light.
right: Please turn on the light.
why: "Open the light" is a direct translation pattern from Hindi and other languages, but in English, lights and electronic devices are 'turned on/off', not 'opened/closed'. Use 'turn on/off' specifically for lights, fans, and electronic devices.

wrong: Please close the light.
right: Please turn off the light.
why: Just like the previous example, this should use 'turn off' rather than 'close' for lights and similar devices. Keep grouping these together: turn on/off, not open/close, for lights and electronics.

wrong: I will do it in two-two days.
right: I will do it in a couple of days. / I will do it in two days.
why: Repeating a number ("two-two") for emphasis is a pattern from Indian languages, but standard English doesn't repeat numbers this way - simply state the number once. Say the number just once, or use 'a couple of' to mean approximately two.

wrong: He is doing timepass.
right: He is relaxing. / He is passing the time.
why: "Timepass" is a term commonly used in Indian English (borrowed and adapted into a single word) but isn't standard international English - use 'relaxing', 'passing the time', or 'killing time' instead. Replace uniquely Indian English terms with their standard international equivalents for clearer global communication.

wrong: I stay in Mumbai.
right: I live in Mumbai.
why: In Indian English, 'stay' is often used to mean 'live' permanently, but in international English, 'stay' usually means a temporary visit (like staying at a hotel), while 'live' means your permanent residence. Use 'live' for where you permanently reside, and reserve 'stay' for temporary visits.

wrong: She is my cousin brother.
right: He is my cousin. / She is my cousin.
why: "Cousin brother" and "cousin sister" are phrases used in Indian English to specify gender, but in standard English, 'cousin' alone doesn't indicate gender, and adding 'brother/sister' is considered nonstandard. Simply say 'cousin' without adding 'brother' or 'sister' - English doesn't distinguish cousin gender this way.

wrong: I have a doubt.
right: I have a question.
why: In Indian English, 'doubt' is commonly used to mean 'question', but in international English, 'doubt' specifically means uncertainty or disbelief about something, not simply a question you want to ask. Use 'question' when you want to ask something, and reserve 'doubt' for genuine uncertainty or skepticism.

wrong: Please give one missed call.
right: Please give me a call. / Please call me and hang up.
why: "Missed call" as a verb phrase ("give a missed call") is a uniquely Indian English usage describing calling briefly and hanging up before it's answered - this specific practice and phrase isn't widely understood internationally. If explaining this practice to an international audience, describe it fully rather than assuming the phrase is understood.

wrong: I am understanding the concept now.
right: I understand the concept now.
why: 'Understand' is a state verb, so it should stay in simple present, not continuous form. This exact mistake pattern (state verbs in continuous form) appears extremely frequently in Indian English, so it's worth mastering thoroughly.

wrong: He is having a lot of tension.
right: He is very stressed. / He has a lot of stress.
why: In Indian English, 'tension' is often used to mean 'stress' or 'worry', but in international English, 'tension' more specifically refers to physical or interpersonal strain (tension in a rope, tension between two people). Use 'stress' or 'worry' to describe someone's mental/emotional state, saving 'tension' for its more specific meanings.

wrong: Don't take tension.
right: Don't worry. / Don't stress.
why: Just like the previous example, 'tension' here should be replaced with 'worry' or 'stress' for clearer, more standard English. Practice this specific phrase correction since it's extremely common in everyday Indian English conversation.

wrong: I am agreeing with you.
right: I agree with you.
why: 'Agree' is a regular verb expressing an opinion or state, and it's not typically used in continuous form when stating agreement. Stick with simple present ('I agree') rather than continuous form for expressing opinions and agreement.

wrong: Prepone the meeting to Monday.
right: Move the meeting earlier to Monday. / Reschedule the meeting to an earlier date.
why: "Prepone" (the opposite of 'postpone') is a word used in Indian English but isn't standard or widely recognized in international English - use 'move earlier' or 'reschedule' instead. While logically clever, 'prepone' should be replaced with standard phrasing for international communication.

wrong: He is out of station.
right: He is out of town. / He is away.
why: "Out of station" is a phrase from Indian English (originally from railway terminology) meaning someone is traveling or away - international English uses 'out of town' or simply 'away'. Replace 'out of station' with 'out of town' for clearer communication with international audiences.

wrong: I will be reaching by 5 PM.
right: I will arrive by 5 PM. / I will reach there by 5 PM (also acceptable, but 'arrive' is more standard).
why: While 'reach' can be used this way, 'arrive' is the more standard, widely recognized verb for this meaning in international English. Consider using 'arrive' as your primary choice, especially in formal writing, though 'reach' is understood too.

wrong: Please share your good self's feedback.
right: Please share your feedback.
why: "Good self" is an overly formal, indirect way of saying 'you' that's specific to certain Indian English business writing traditions, but international English simply uses 'you' or 'your' directly. Simplify formal, indirect phrases like 'your good self' to the direct 'you' for clearer modern business writing.

wrong: I am a fresher, looking for my first job.
right: I am a recent graduate, looking for my first job. / I am new to the workforce.
why: "Fresher" is commonly used in Indian English (and Indian job markets) to mean someone new to the workforce, but international English typically uses 'recent graduate' or 'entry-level candidate'. Use 'recent graduate' or 'entry-level' in international resumes and job applications instead of 'fresher'.

wrong: He is a good boy, no?
right: He is a good boy, isn't he?
why: Ending a statement with "no?" as a general question tag is common in some Indian English speech patterns, but standard English uses a proper question tag matching the sentence's verb (isn't he, doesn't he, can't he). Use the correctly matched question tag based on the main verb, rather than a generic "no?" or "isn't it?" for every sentence.

wrong: This is very much correct, isn't it?
right: This is quite correct, isn't it? / This is absolutely correct, isn't it?
why: "Very much correct" is a slightly unusual intensifier combination in Indian English - 'quite', 'absolutely', or simply 'very' work better as standard intensifiers before 'correct'. Choose standard intensifiers (very, quite, absolutely) rather than combining 'very much' before an adjective like this.

wrong: I have completed my graduation.
right: I have completed my degree. / I have graduated.
why: In Indian English, 'graduation' sometimes refers to the degree itself (bachelor's degree), while in international English, 'graduation' specifically means the ceremony or event of completing your studies. Use 'degree' to refer to your qualification, and 'graduation' only for the ceremony or moment of completing it.

wrong: I got a cold since yesterday.
right: I have had a cold since yesterday.
why: Since this describes something starting in the past and continuing to now (marked by 'since'), it needs present perfect ('have had'), not simple past ('got'). Whenever 'since' appears with a starting point, use present perfect tense, not simple past.

wrong: I stay with my family in Delhi.
right: I live with my family in Delhi.
why: This repeats the stay/live distinction covered earlier - since this describes a permanent living situation, 'live' is the correct choice, not 'stay'. Keep reinforcing this distinction: live = permanent residence, stay = temporary visit.

wrong: He is a nice person, actually.
right: He is a nice person.
why: While not incorrect, the word "actually" is used as a filler word extremely frequently in Indian English speech, often without adding real meaning - consider whether it's truly needed in each sentence. Use 'actually' sparingly and only when genuinely contrasting with an expectation, rather than as a habitual filler word.

wrong: I am having a headache since morning.
right: I have had a headache since morning. / I have a headache.
why: This combines two mistakes: 'have' for physical symptoms shouldn't be in continuous form, and if using 'since', the tense should be present perfect ('have had'), not present continuous. Physical states and symptoms (headache, fever, pain) use simple 'have' or present perfect 'have had', never continuous form.

wrong: Give me one minute, I am coming.
right: Give me a minute, I'm coming. / One moment, please.
why: While understandable, "one minute" is slightly unusual phrasing compared to the more standard 'a minute' or 'a moment' in international English. Prefer 'a minute/moment' over 'one minute' for smoother, more natural-sounding English.

wrong: I need to purchase some grocery.
right: I need to buy some groceries.
why: 'Grocery' items are referred to as 'groceries' (plural) in standard English, and 'buy' is more commonly used in everyday speech than the more formal 'purchase'. Use 'groceries' (plural) for shopping items, and 'buy' for everyday casual speech.

wrong: This is a hi-fi restaurant.
right: This is a fancy/upscale restaurant.
why: "Hi-fi" (meaning 'high quality' or 'fancy') is used informally in Indian English but isn't standard international usage - 'hi-fi' internationally refers only to high-fidelity audio equipment. Replace 'hi-fi' meaning 'fancy' with 'upscale', 'fancy', or 'high-end' for international clarity.

wrong: I completed my schooling from Delhi Public School.
right: I completed my schooling at Delhi Public School.
why: The preposition 'at' is used with specific institution names (school, university), not 'from' - 'from' is reserved for describing where you graduated or the source of something. Use 'at' when referring to studying at a specific institution, and 'from' when describing where you graduated.

wrong: I am not able to do this work.
right: I can't do this work. / I'm unable to do this work.
why: While grammatically correct, "not able to" is a longer, less natural way of expressing inability compared to the more common 'can't' or 'unable to'. Prefer the more concise, natural 'can't' or 'unable to' over the longer 'not able to' in everyday speech and writing.

wrong: Kindly revert with your valuable feedback.
right: Please share your feedback.
why: This combines two commonly flagged Indian business English phrases: "kindly revert" and "valuable feedback" - simplify to direct, natural phrasing. Avoid stacking multiple formal, indirect phrases together in professional communication; state your request plainly and directly.

wrong: This is not up to the mark.
right: This isn't up to standard. / This doesn't meet expectations.
why: "Up to the mark" is understood in some varieties of English but 'up to standard' or 'meets expectations' are more universally recognized alternatives in international business contexts. Consider using more globally recognized phrases in professional writing meant for diverse, international audiences.

wrong: I want to become an engineer, so that I can serve the society.
right: I want to become an engineer so that I can serve society.
why: In this general sense (society as a whole concept), 'society' doesn't need 'the' in front of it - this is similar to how we don't say 'the life' for life in general. Drop 'the' when referring to society, life, or nature as broad, general concepts rather than one specific instance.

wrong: We are like family only.
right: We are like family.
why: Adding "only" at the end for emphasis is a common pattern borrowed from Indian languages, but it's not needed in standard English and can sound repetitive or unclear to international readers. Simply end the sentence without 'only' when it's not logically limiting anything specific.

wrong: I have two-three items to discuss.
right: I have two or three items to discuss. / I have a few items to discuss.
why: Similar to "two-two days", combining two numbers with a hyphen ("two-three") is a pattern from Indian languages - standard English uses 'or' between the numbers, or simply 'a few'. Use 'two or three' or 'a few' instead of hyphenating two numbers together.

wrong: Please find the attachment herewith.
right: Please find the attachment attached. / I've attached the file.
why: "Herewith" is a very formal, old-fashioned word rarely used in modern international business English - simpler, more natural phrasing is preferred today. Replace old-fashioned formal words like 'herewith' with simpler modern equivalents in everyday professional writing.

wrong: I am understanding your concern.
right: I understand your concern.
why: This repeats the classic state-verb continuous mistake one final time - 'understand' should always stay in simple present, never continuous form. By now, this pattern should feel completely automatic: state verbs (know, understand, want, like, believe) never take '-ing'.

wrong: He is having good English.
right: He has good English. / He speaks English well.
why: 'Have' describing a skill or quality is a state, not an action, so it stays in simple present ('has'), never continuous ('is having'). This is the same core state-verb rule applied once more to a skill description rather than a physical possession.

wrong: What is the timing of the shop?
right: What are the shop's hours? / When does the shop open and close?
why: "Timing" (singular) used this way to ask about opening hours is common in Indian English, but international English uses 'hours' (plural) or asks directly about opening/closing times. Use 'hours' or ask directly about opening and closing times instead of 'timing' for this meaning.

wrong: Walking to school, the rain started falling.
right: While I was walking to school, the rain started falling.
why: As written, 'walking to school' seems to describe 'the rain', which doesn't make sense - rain doesn't walk. Add a subject who is actually doing the walking, either by rewriting with 'while I was walking' or restructuring the sentence entirely.

wrong: After finishing the assignment, the teacher collected our papers.
right: After we finished the assignment, the teacher collected our papers.
why: This sentence implies the TEACHER finished the assignment, when really it was the students - the modifier is describing the wrong person. Make sure the subject right after the modifier phrase is the one who actually performed that action.

wrong: Covered in chocolate, I ate the cake.
right: I ate the cake covered in chocolate.
why: As written, 'covered in chocolate' seems to describe the person (I), not the cake, which is a strange and unintended image. Move the modifier closer to what it's actually describing - the cake, not the eater.

wrong: Barking loudly, the mailman was scared by the dog.
right: Barking loudly, the dog scared the mailman.
why: The phrase 'barking loudly' should describe the dog, but the sentence structure makes it seem to describe the mailman, which doesn't make sense. Place the true subject of the action (the dog) immediately after the modifier.

wrong: Excited about the trip, the bags were packed quickly.
right: Excited about the trip, she packed the bags quickly.
why: Bags can't feel excitement - a person can, so the sentence needs a human subject right after the modifier. Always ask: who or what can actually feel or do what the modifier describes?

wrong: Tired after a long day, the bed felt very comfortable.
right: Tired after a long day, I found the bed very comfortable.
why: The bed can't feel tired - only a person can, so the true subject (I, she, he) needs to follow the modifier directly. This is one of the most common dangling modifier patterns, worth watching for carefully.

wrong: I only ate two slices of the pizza.
right: I ate only two slices of the pizza.
why: This is a misplaced modifier - 'only' is currently placed to suggest 'the only thing I did was eat' (rather than share, buy, etc.), when the intended meaning is limiting the NUMBER of slices. Move 'only' directly before the word it's meant to limit - here, 'two slices'.

wrong: She almost drove her kids to school every day.
right: She drove her kids to school almost every day.
why: As written, 'almost drove' suggests she nearly had an accident or nearly didn't drive at all, but the intended meaning is that this happened on ALMOST every day (meaning frequently, but not quite always). Move 'almost' right before 'every day' to fix the intended meaning.

wrong: I just want to say one thing to you.
right: I want to say just one thing to you.
why: Placing 'just' before 'want' suggests a limitation on wanting itself, but the intended meaning is limiting the number of things you want to say (just one). Move 'just' directly before the word it's actually limiting - 'one thing'.

wrong: He nearly failed all of his classes.
right: He failed nearly all of his classes.
why: As written, 'nearly failed' suggests he almost failed but didn't, while the intended meaning is that he failed almost ALL of his classes (meaning most, but not literally every one). Move 'nearly' right before 'all' to express the intended meaning about quantity.

wrong: The dog only barks at strangers.
right: The dog barks only at strangers.
why: As written, 'only barks' could mean barking is the only thing the dog does (compared to other actions), but the intended meaning is that strangers are the only thing it barks AT. Move 'only' right before 'at strangers' to clarify the intended limitation.

wrong: We nearly watched the entire movie before falling asleep.
right: We watched nearly the entire movie before falling asleep.
why: 'Nearly watched' suggests almost starting to watch, but the intended meaning is watching almost the ENTIRE movie (most of it, but not literally all). Move 'nearly' before 'the entire movie' to express the correct meaning about quantity watched.

wrong: She served sandwiches to the children on paper plates.
right: She served sandwiches on paper plates to the children.
why: As written, it sounds like the children were on the paper plates, when really the sandwiches were served on paper plates. Move the modifier ('on paper plates') closer to the word it's actually describing - sandwiches, not children.

wrong: I saw a beautiful sunset driving to work.
right: While driving to work, I saw a beautiful sunset.
why: As written, this sentence could suggest the sunset was driving to work, which is impossible. Clarify by placing 'while driving to work' at the beginning, clearly connected to 'I'.

wrong: The waiter brought a plate of pasta to the customer covered in cheese.
right: The waiter brought the customer a plate of pasta covered in cheese.
why: As written, it sounds like the CUSTOMER is covered in cheese, when really the pasta is. Move the modifier phrase ('covered in cheese') right after the word it describes - pasta, not customer.

wrong: I gave my brother a book that I borrowed from the library on his birthday.
right: On his birthday, I gave my brother a book that I borrowed from the library.
why: As written, this sentence is confusingly ordered, making it seem like the birthday and the library visit are connected when they aren't clearly separated. Move 'on his birthday' closer to the beginning or right after 'gave my brother' for clarity.

wrong: I read an article about earthquakes in the newspaper this morning.
right: This morning, I read an article about earthquakes in the newspaper.
why: While this sentence isn't strictly incorrect, moving the time phrase 'this morning' to the front makes the sentence flow better and reduces the pile-up of descriptive phrases at the end. Consider spreading out multiple modifiers rather than stacking them all at the end of a sentence.

wrong: I almost spent all my savings on a new laptop.
right: I spent almost all my savings on a new laptop.
why: 'Almost spent' suggests you nearly made a purchase but didn't, while the intended meaning is spending almost ALL your savings (most, not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'all' to correctly show the amount spent.

wrong: He only speaks to me when he needs a favor.
right: He speaks to me only when he needs a favor.
why: As written, 'only speaks' could mean speaking is the only action he takes (versus writing, calling, etc.), while the intended meaning limits WHEN he speaks to you. Move 'only' directly before 'when' to clarify the timing restriction.

wrong: I bought a shirt for my father with a floral pattern.
right: I bought a shirt with a floral pattern for my father.
why: As written, it sounds like the FATHER has a floral pattern, when really the shirt does. Move the modifier ('with a floral pattern') closer to what it's describing - the shirt, not the father.

wrong: The manager offered a solution to the problem that was practical.
right: The manager offered a practical solution to the problem.
why: As written, it's unclear whether 'that was practical' describes the solution or the problem. Move the modifier ('practical') directly before the word it clearly describes - solution.

wrong: I saw a man walking my dog.
right: While walking my dog, I saw a man. / I saw a man who was walking a dog.
why: This sentence is ambiguous - it's unclear whether the SPEAKER or the MAN was walking the dog. Rewrite so it's completely clear who is actually doing the walking.

wrong: She gave the medicine to her son that tasted terrible.
right: She gave her son the medicine that tasted terrible.
why: As written, it sounds like the SON tastes terrible, when really the medicine does. Move the descriptive clause right next to the word it describes - medicine, not son.

wrong: He nearly ate the whole pizza by himself.
right: He ate nearly the whole pizza by himself.
why: 'Nearly ate' suggests he almost started eating but didn't, while the intended meaning is eating almost the WHOLE pizza. Move 'nearly' before 'the whole pizza' to express the correct meaning about quantity eaten.

wrong: I bought a new phone from the store that has a great camera.
right: I bought a new phone that has a great camera from the store.
why: As written, it sounds like the STORE has a great camera, when really the phone does. Move the descriptive clause directly after the word it's meant to describe.

wrong: The company hired an employee with excellent references quickly.
right: The company quickly hired an employee with excellent references.
why: As written, 'quickly' at the end could seem to describe the references rather than the hiring process. Move 'quickly' closer to the verb it's actually describing - hired.

wrong: I only drink coffee in the morning.
right: I drink coffee only in the morning.
why: As written, 'only drink' could suggest drinking is the only thing you do with coffee (versus buying, smelling, etc.), while the intended meaning limits WHEN you drink it. Move 'only' directly before 'in the morning' to clarify the timing.

wrong: She almost cooked dinner for the entire family.
right: She cooked dinner for almost the entire family.
why: 'Almost cooked' suggests she nearly started cooking but didn't, while the intended meaning is cooking for almost the ENTIRE family (most, not all). Move 'almost' before 'the entire family' to show the correct meaning about who was served.

wrong: I only need five minutes to finish this.
right: I need only five minutes to finish this.
why: As written, 'only need' could suggest needing is the only thing happening (versus wanting, requesting, etc.), while the intended meaning limits the AMOUNT of time. Move 'only' directly before 'five minutes' to clarify the limitation on quantity.

wrong: She wrapped a gift for her friend in colorful paper.
right: She wrapped a gift in colorful paper for her friend.
why: As written, it sounds like the FRIEND is wrapped in colorful paper, when really the gift is. Move the modifier phrase directly after the word it's meant to describe.

wrong: He almost finished reading the entire book in one day.
right: He finished reading almost the entire book in one day.
why: 'Almost finished' suggests he nearly completed the task but didn't, while the intended meaning is finishing almost the WHOLE book (most, not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'the entire book' for the correct meaning.

wrong: The store sells clothes for children made of organic cotton.
right: The store sells clothes made of organic cotton for children.
why: As written, it sounds like the CHILDREN are made of organic cotton, when really the clothes are. Move the modifier directly after 'clothes', the word it should describe.

wrong: I gave a present to my sister wrapped in gold paper.
right: I gave my sister a present wrapped in gold paper.
why: As written, it sounds like the SISTER is wrapped in gold paper, when really the present is. Rearrange so the modifier sits right next to the present, not the sister.

wrong: We nearly drove the entire distance without stopping.
right: We drove nearly the entire distance without stopping.
why: 'Nearly drove' suggests almost starting the drive but not quite, while the intended meaning is driving almost the WHOLE distance. Move 'nearly' before 'the entire distance' for the correct meaning.

wrong: The librarian recommended a book to the student that won several awards.
right: The librarian recommended a book that won several awards to the student.
why: As written, it's ambiguous whether the book or the student won awards. Move the descriptive clause ('that won several awards') directly next to 'book', the word it should describe.

wrong: I only saw her once at the conference.
right: I saw her only once at the conference.
why: As written, 'only saw' could suggest seeing is the only thing that happened (versus talking, meeting, etc.), while the intended meaning limits the NUMBER of times. Move 'only' directly before 'once' to clarify the correct limitation.

wrong: She almost spoke to every person at the party.
right: She spoke to almost every person at the party.
why: 'Almost spoke' suggests she nearly began speaking but didn't, while the intended meaning is speaking to almost EVERY person (most, but not literally all). Move 'almost' before 'every person' for the correct meaning about quantity.

wrong: I only bought this for you, not for anyone else.
right: I bought this only for you, not for anyone else.
why: As written, 'only bought' could suggest buying is the only action taken, while the intended meaning limits WHO it was bought for. Move 'only' directly before 'for you' to clarify the intended limitation.

wrong: The company delivered a package to the customer damaged in shipping.
right: The company delivered a package damaged in shipping to the customer.
why: As written, it sounds like the CUSTOMER is damaged, when really the package is. Move the modifier phrase ('damaged in shipping') directly next to 'package', the word it's meant to describe.

wrong: I not like this movie.
right: I don't like this movie.
why: In present simple, negatives need the helping verb 'do' plus 'not' (don't), not just 'not' placed directly before the verb. Always build negatives with: subject + don't/doesn't + base verb.

wrong: She not likes coffee.
right: She doesn't like coffee.
why: With he/she/it, the negative uses 'doesn't' (not 'don't'), and the main verb returns to its base form without the 's'. Remember: doesn't already carries the 's', so the main verb stays plain.

wrong: I no want to go.
right: I don't want to go.
why: 'No' is used before nouns to show absence (no money, no time), but it can't be used to negate a verb directly - verbs need 'don't/doesn't/didn't' instead. Keep 'no' for nouns, and 'don't/doesn't' for verbs.

wrong: He no goes to school.
right: He doesn't go to school.
why: Just like the previous example, 'no' cannot negate a verb - use 'doesn't' plus the base verb form instead. This mistake is very common among speakers whose first language uses a simple negative word before any part of speech.

wrong: I didn't went there.
right: I didn't go there.
why: Once 'didn't' is used, the main verb must return to its base form ('go'), not stay in the past tense ('went'). Remember: didn't already shows the past tense, so the main verb doesn't need to show it again.

wrong: She didn't wanted to come.
right: She didn't want to come.
why: Just like the previous example, 'didn't' requires the base form of the verb afterward, not the past tense form. This is one of the most frequent negative sentence mistakes, worth practicing repeatedly until automatic.

wrong: I am not knowing the answer.
right: I don't know the answer.
why: This combines two separate mistakes: 'know' is a state verb that shouldn't be in continuous form, and the negative for present simple state verbs uses 'don't', not 'am not' plus '-ing'. Always negate state verbs with don't/doesn't, never am/is/are + not + -ing.

wrong: I have not any money.
right: I don't have any money. / I have no money.
why: While 'have not' can work in very formal British English, the standard modern form uses 'don't have' or simply 'have no'. For everyday writing, choose 'don't have any' or 'have no' rather than 'have not any'.

wrong: I can not to swim.
right: I can't swim. / I cannot swim.
why: This sentence has two errors: 'to' should never follow a modal verb like 'can', and 'can not' is usually written as one word, 'cannot' (or contracted as 'can't'). Remember: modal + not + base verb, with no 'to' in between.

wrong: I don't can help you.
right: I can't help you.
why: Modal verbs like 'can' form their own negative directly (can't), and should never be combined with 'don't'. Whenever a sentence has a modal verb, negate the modal itself, not with an extra 'don't'.

wrong: She don't has time today.
right: She doesn't have time today.
why: With he/she/it, you need 'doesn't', not 'don't' - and the main verb 'has' must return to its base form 'have'. Double-check both the helping verb agreement and the base form of the main verb together.

wrong: They doesn't like the plan.
right: They don't like the plan.
why: With 'they' (plural), you need 'don't', not 'doesn't', which is reserved for he/she/it only. Match the negative helping verb to the subject just as carefully as you would in a positive sentence.

wrong: I am not have a car.
right: I don't have a car.
why: Possession with 'have' uses 'don't/doesn't' for negation, not 'am/is/are not'. Keep this straight: 'have' meaning possession follows the do/does pattern for negatives, not the be-verb pattern.

wrong: It is not working good.
right: It isn't working well. / It doesn't work well.
why: This has two issues: use 'well' (adverb) instead of 'good' (adjective) to describe how something works, and check whether you mean a temporary state ('isn't working' - broken right now) or a general truth ('doesn't work' - never functions properly). Choose your tense and word form carefully based on the intended meaning.

wrong: I wasn't seen him yesterday.
right: I didn't see him yesterday.
why: This mixes up two different tenses incorrectly - for a simple, one-time past action, use 'didn't' plus the base verb, not 'wasn't' plus the past participle (which would be used for other structures like passive voice). Use didn't + base verb for straightforward negative statements about the past.

wrong: I am not agree with this.
right: I don't agree with this. / I disagree with this.
why: 'Agree' is a regular action verb, not something used with 'am/is/are' - negate it with 'don't/doesn't' instead. Alternatively, you can use the opposite verb 'disagree' for a cleaner, more direct statement.

wrong: I not have seen this movie.
right: I haven't seen this movie.
why: This present perfect negative needs 'haven't' (have + not, contracted), not 'not have' in that order. Keep the correct order: subject + haven't/hasn't + past participle.

wrong: She hasn't saw that show.
right: She hasn't seen that show.
why: After 'hasn't', you need the past participle form of the verb ('seen'), not the simple past form ('saw'). This follows the same pattern as positive present perfect: have/has + past participle, whether positive or negative.

wrong: I don't have never been there.
right: I have never been there.
why: This sentence combines two negative-related words incorrectly - 'don't have' and 'never' shouldn't be combined this way; simply use 'have never' with present perfect. Remove 'don't' entirely when using 'never' with present perfect tense.

wrong: I am not sure not about this.
right: I am not sure about this.
why: This sentence has an accidental double 'not', which is simply a proofreading slip - remove the extra negative word. Always double-check your negative sentences for accidentally repeated negative words.

wrong: He isn't never late.
right: He is never late. / He isn't ever late.
why: 'Isn't' and 'never' are both negative, creating an unintended double negative - choose either the positive 'is' with 'never', or 'isn't' with 'ever'. This is the same double-negative pattern covered in an earlier lesson, appearing again in a slightly different form.

wrong: I not want any trouble.
right: I don't want any trouble.
why: As with other present simple negatives, this needs 'don't' before the base verb, not 'not' placed directly in front of it. Keep building this pattern until it's fully automatic: subject + don't/doesn't + base verb.

wrong: I don't have to went there.
right: I don't have to go there.
why: After 'don't have to' (meaning something isn't necessary), the main verb must stay in its base form ('go'), not the past tense. Remember: 'have to' and its negative 'don't have to' are always followed by the base verb.

wrong: She won't goes to the party.
right: She won't go to the party.
why: After 'won't' (will not), the main verb must stay in base form ('go'), not take an 's' ending. This follows the same rule as all modal verbs: modal + base verb, with no extra endings.

wrong: I shouldn't went there alone.
right: I shouldn't have gone there alone. / I shouldn't go there alone.
why: This sentence mixes up tenses - if you're talking about a past mistake, use 'shouldn't have gone' (regret about the past); if talking about future advice, use 'shouldn't go' (simple base verb). Choose the structure based on whether you're regretting a past action or advising about a future one.

wrong: He didn't used to smoke.
right: He didn't use to smoke.
why: After 'didn't', the verb 'use to' loses its 'd' ending, since 'didn't' already carries the past tense meaning. This is a subtle spelling rule: didn't + use to (no d), not didn't + used to.

wrong: I am not understanding this lesson.
right: I don't understand this lesson.
why: 'Understand' is a state verb and isn't typically used in continuous form, even in the negative - use simple present negative instead. Stick with 'don't/doesn't understand' for state verbs like this one.

wrong: They not were at home.
right: They weren't at home.
why: With 'be' verbs in the past tense, the negative is formed by adding 'not' directly after the verb (were + not = weren't), not by placing 'not' before the subject or verb separately. Keep the correct order: subject + wasn't/weren't + rest of sentence.

wrong: I not was ready.
right: I wasn't ready.
why: Just like the previous example, the negative of 'was' is formed by adding 'not' right after it (wasn't), not by placing 'not' elsewhere in the sentence. Always attach 'not' directly to the be-verb it's negating.

wrong: We not have finished yet.
right: We haven't finished yet.
why: This present perfect negative needs 'haven't' (have + not), with the correct word order, not 'not have' reversed. Keep repeating this pattern until it becomes automatic: subject + haven't/hasn't + past participle.

wrong: I hope you not worry about this.
right: I hope you don't worry about this.
why: This present simple negative needs 'don't' before the verb, not 'not' placed directly before it without a helping verb. Always insert 'don't/doesn't' for present simple negatives, even in more complex sentences like this one.

wrong: It doesn't matters to me.
right: It doesn't matter to me.
why: Once 'doesn't' is in place, the main verb must return to its base form ('matter'), not keep the 's' ending ('matters'). Remember: doesn't already includes the 's' meaning, so the verb itself stays plain.

wrong: I no longer I like this song.
right: I no longer like this song.
why: This sentence accidentally repeats the subject 'I' - 'no longer' already modifies the verb directly, without needing the subject restated. Remove the second 'I' since the sentence only needs one subject.

wrong: Not anyone came to the party.
right: No one came to the party. / Not anyone came to the party (less common but acceptable).
why: While technically both forms can work, 'no one' is the much more natural, standard way to express this idea in English. Prefer 'no one' or 'nobody' as your default choice for this kind of negative statement.

wrong: I ain't got no time for this.
right: I don't have any time for this. / I have no time for this.
why: 'Ain't' is a non-standard, informal contraction not used in formal writing, and combined with 'no' here, it also creates a double negative. For standard English, use 'don't have any' or 'have no', avoiding 'ain't' entirely in formal contexts.

wrong: I can't hardly wait for tomorrow.
right: I can hardly wait for tomorrow.
why: Since 'hardly' already carries a negative-like meaning (meaning 'barely'), combining it with 'can't' creates an unintended double negative. Use 'hardly' with a positive verb (can, will, is), never with a negative one.

wrong: There is not many people here.
right: There aren't many people here. / There are not many people here.
why: Since 'people' is plural, the negative needs 'aren't' (are + not), matching the plural subject, not the singular 'is not'. Check subject-verb agreement in negative sentences just as carefully as in positive ones.

wrong: I couldn't never understand this topic.
right: I could never understand this topic. / I couldn't ever understand this topic.
why: 'Couldn't' and 'never' are both negative, creating a double negative - choose either the positive 'could' with 'never', or 'couldn't' with 'ever'. This double-negative pattern with modal verbs follows the same rule as with other negative words.

wrong: I am not certain not if this works.
right: I am not certain if this works.
why: This sentence has an accidental double 'not' from careless writing - remove the extra one. Proofreading carefully for accidentally repeated negative words prevents this kind of mistake.

wrong: He didn't wanted anything to eat.
right: He didn't want anything to eat.
why: As covered before, 'didn't' requires the base verb form afterward ('want'), never the past tense form ('wanted'). This mistake pattern is worth memorizing thoroughly since it appears so frequently.

wrong: I don't know nothing anymore.
right: I don't know anything anymore.
why: This is the classic double negative pattern - 'don't' and 'nothing' together cancel out logically, so use 'anything' after the negative verb instead. This exact mistake type has appeared in earlier lessons too, showing how common and important it is to master.

wrong: We aren't leaving not yet.
right: We aren't leaving yet.
why: This sentence has an unnecessary extra 'not' since 'aren't' already makes the sentence negative - adding 'not' again is a duplicate negative. Keep only one negative marker per clause; here, 'aren't' alone is sufficient.

wrong: I don't want to not go.
right: I don't want to go. / I want to not go, but I have no choice (rare, specific meaning).
why: In almost all everyday contexts, simply saying 'I don't want to go' correctly expresses not wanting to attend - stacking 'don't want' with an additional 'not' before 'go' creates confusing, doubled negation. Stick with the simpler, single-negative version unless you have a very specific reason to emphasize refusal to not do something.

wrong: They weren't never on time for meetings.
right: They were never on time for meetings. / They weren't ever on time for meetings.
why: 'Weren't' and 'never' are both negative, forming a double negative - choose either the positive 'were' with 'never', or 'weren't' with 'ever'. This is the same double-negative rule appearing once more with the past tense of 'be'.

wrong: I haven't no idea what happened.
right: I have no idea what happened. / I haven't any idea what happened.
why: 'Haven't' and 'no' together create a double negative - choose either the positive 'have' with 'no', or the negative 'haven't' with 'any'. This common phrase is worth practicing correctly since it appears so often in daily conversation.

wrong: It don't matter to me.
right: It doesn't matter to me.
why: Since 'it' is singular (like he/she), it needs 'doesn't', not 'don't', which is reserved for I/you/we/they. This is one of the most common subject-verb agreement mistakes in negative sentences, especially in casual speech.

wrong: I no longer don't smoke.
right: I no longer smoke. / I don't smoke anymore.
why: This sentence combines two ways of expressing the same negative idea, creating an accidental double negative - choose either 'no longer' with a positive verb, or 'don't...anymore' with a negative verb. Pick one clean structure to express that a habit has stopped.

wrong: I am good in English.
right: I am good at English.
why: In English, the adjective 'good' always pairs with the preposition 'at' when talking about a skill or subject - this is simply a fixed combination you need to memorize, not something you can work out logically. Whenever you talk about being skilled at something, reach for 'good at', never 'good in'.

wrong: We reached to the station.
right: We reached the station.
why: The verb 'reach' already means 'arrive at', so it goes directly before the place with no preposition needed at all. Adding 'to' after 'reach' is one of the most common mistakes among learners, so it's worth practicing the verb without any preposition.

wrong: Let's discuss about this.
right: Let's discuss this.
why: 'Discuss' already carries the meaning of 'talk about', so adding the word 'about' afterward is unnecessary repetition. Think of 'discuss' as a verb that swallows 'about' inside its own meaning.

wrong: I am married with her.
right: I am married to her.
why: The fixed expression in English is 'married to' someone, not 'married with' - this is simply how native speakers always say it. Practice the phrase as one unit: 'married to', so it becomes automatic.

wrong: She is angry on me.
right: She is angry with me.
why: When you're angry AT a person, English uses 'angry with' (or 'angry at'); 'angry on' is not correct in any context. Use 'angry about' instead when you're upset about a situation rather than a person.

wrong: He is afraid from dogs.
right: He is afraid of dogs.
why: The fixed pairing is 'afraid of', describing what causes the fear - 'afraid from' doesn't exist in standard English. If you're learning this for the first time, just remember: fear is always 'of' something.

wrong: I am interested about music.
right: I am interested in music.
why: 'Interested' always pairs with 'in', showing what topic draws your attention - 'interested about' is a common but incorrect substitution. A simple memory aid: your interest goes 'in' something, like stepping into a topic.

wrong: She depends of her parents.
right: She depends on her parents.
why: The verb 'depend' is always followed by 'on' to show who or what you rely on. This is one of the easiest fixed pairs to memorize since 'depend on' rhymes nicely and sticks in memory.

wrong: He is capable to do this.
right: He is capable of doing this.
why: 'Capable' pairs with 'of', and it must be followed by the -ing form of the verb (doing), not the plain infinitive (to do). Remember the full pattern together: 'capable of' + verb-ing.

wrong: I congratulate you for your success.
right: I congratulate you on your success.
why: The verb 'congratulate' pairs with 'on', not 'for', when mentioning the achievement being celebrated. Practice the set phrase 'congratulate someone on something' as a whole unit.

wrong: Compare this to that book.
right: Compare this with that book.
why: When you want to point out both similarities and differences between two things, 'compare with' is the safer, more standard choice ('compare to' is used specifically when highlighting a resemblance, like comparing a rose to love). For most everyday comparisons, 'with' is the reliable option.

wrong: He apologized for his mistake to me.
right: He apologized to me for his mistake.
why: The natural word order in English is: apologize + TO the person + FOR the reason. Keeping this order sounds much more natural to native speakers, even though your original meaning was already clear.

wrong: I am proud about my daughter.
right: I am proud of my daughter.
why: 'Proud' always pairs with 'of' to show what or who you feel proud about. This is a very common fixed pairing, so practice saying 'proud of' as one single phrase.

wrong: She is different than her sister.
right: She is different from her sister.
why: In careful, formal English, 'different from' is the standard and universally accepted form ('different than' is common in casual American English, but 'different from' is safer in writing and exams). When in doubt, choose 'different from'.

wrong: I will explain you the process.
right: I will explain the process to you.
why: The verb 'explain' cannot be followed directly by a person - you must add 'to' before the person receiving the explanation. Remember the pattern: explain something TO someone, never explain someone something.

wrong: He is senior than me.
right: He is senior to me.
why: Unlike most comparative adjectives that use 'than' (taller than, faster than), 'senior' and 'junior' are exceptions that pair with 'to'. These two words are worth memorizing separately since they break the usual comparison pattern.

wrong: I graduated in college last year.
right: I graduated from college last year.
why: The fixed expression is 'graduate from' an institution, showing where you completed your studies. Picture yourself moving 'from' the school out into the world - that image can help the preposition stick.

wrong: She was absent for the meeting.
right: She was absent from the meeting.
why: 'Absent' pairs with 'from', showing the place or event someone was missing from. This is a simple fixed pairing worth memorizing directly: absent FROM, not absent FOR.

wrong: He insisted for going alone.
right: He insisted on going alone.
why: The verb 'insist' is always followed by 'on' plus the -ing form when describing what someone firmly wants to do. Practice the full pattern: insist ON doing something.

wrong: I am waiting from you.
right: I am waiting for you.
why: 'Wait' pairs with 'for' to show who or what you are expecting. This is one of the most basic and frequently used fixed prepositions in English, so it's worth mastering early.

wrong: She succeeded to pass the exam.
right: She succeeded in passing the exam.
why: 'Succeed' is followed by 'in' plus the -ing form of the verb, not 'to' plus the base verb. Memorize the whole phrase together: succeed IN doing something.

wrong: He is good with maths.
right: He is good at maths.
why: Just like 'good at English' earlier, any subject or skill pairs with 'at', never 'with' or 'in'. Build one simple rule in your mind: good AT + [skill or subject].

wrong: I will arrive to the airport at 6.
right: I will arrive at the airport at 6.
why: 'Arrive' pairs with 'at' for smaller, specific locations like airports, stations, and buildings. Compare this with the next mistake, where cities and countries use a different preposition.

wrong: We will arrive at London tomorrow.
right: We will arrive in London tomorrow.
why: For larger places like cities and countries, 'arrive' pairs with 'in' instead of 'at'. A simple rule: small specific places = arrive AT, big places like cities/countries = arrive IN.

wrong: He shouted on me for no reason.
right: He shouted at me for no reason.
why: When shouting is aimed angrily at a person, English uses 'shout at' ('shout to' is used only when calling out to someone far away in a friendly, neutral way). Since this sentence describes anger, 'at' is correct here.

wrong: I am tired of walk.
right: I am tired of walking.
