why: 'Tired of' must be followed by the -ing form of the verb, not the plain base form. Practice pairing 'tired of' directly with an -ing verb until it feels automatic.

wrong: She is fond about chocolates.
right: She is fond of chocolates.
why: 'Fond' always pairs with 'of' to describe something you like. This is a simple, fixed phrase worth memorizing as one unit: fond OF.

wrong: He was accused for theft.
right: He was accused of theft.
why: The fixed legal and everyday expression is 'accused of' a crime or wrongdoing. Whenever discussing blame or accusation, default to 'of', not 'for'.

wrong: I prevented him to go.
right: I prevented him from going.
why: 'Prevent' is followed by 'from' plus the -ing form, describing what action was stopped. Remember the complete structure: prevent someone FROM doing something.

wrong: She is talented in singing.
right: She is talented at singing.
why: 'Talented' pairs with 'at' to show a specific skill, following the same pattern as 'good at' and 'skilled at'. Group these three words together in memory since they behave the same way: good/talented/skilled AT.

wrong: I look forward to hear from you.
right: I look forward to hearing from you.
why: In the phrase 'look forward to', the word 'to' here is a preposition, so it must be followed by the -ing form, not the base verb. This is a very common mistake because 'to' usually signals an infinitive (to hear) elsewhere in English, but not in this specific phrase.

wrong: He is famous by his voice.
right: He is famous for his voice.
why: 'Famous' pairs with 'for', showing the reason someone is well known. Practice the whole phrase: famous FOR + [reason].

wrong: I am bored of this movie.
right: I am bored with this movie.
why: The more standard, traditional pairing is 'bored with', although 'bored of' has become common in casual speech. For formal writing and exams, choose 'bored with' to be safe.

wrong: She was surprised of the news.
right: She was surprised at the news.
why: 'Surprised' pairs with 'at' (or sometimes 'by'), never 'of'. Whenever describing a reaction of surprise, reach for 'at' as your default choice.

wrong: I am scared with spiders.
right: I am scared of spiders.
why: 'Scared' follows the same pattern as 'afraid' - both pair with 'of' to show the source of fear. Group 'afraid of' and 'scared of' together in your memory since they work identically.

wrong: He participated for the event.
right: He participated in the event.
why: 'Participate' pairs with 'in', showing what activity someone took part in. Remember: you participate IN something, stepping into the activity.

wrong: I believe on you.
right: I believe in you.
why: 'Believe' pairs with 'in' when expressing trust or confidence in a person or idea. This is different from simply 'believe someone' (meaning you accept what they say is true) versus 'believe in someone' (meaning you have faith in their ability).

wrong: She apologized about being late.
right: She apologized for being late.
why: As seen earlier, 'apologize' pairs with 'for' to state the reason for the apology. Keep the full structure in mind: apologize TO someone FOR something.

wrong: He complained for the noise.
right: He complained about the noise.
why: 'Complain' pairs with 'about' to describe the subject of the complaint. This is a very common everyday phrase worth memorizing directly: complain ABOUT.

wrong: I concentrate in my studies.
right: I concentrate on my studies.
why: 'Concentrate' pairs with 'on', showing where your focus and attention are directed. Picture your attention landing 'on' a target - that image can help this preposition stick in memory.

wrong: She invested her money on stocks.
right: She invested her money in stocks.
why: 'Invest' pairs with 'in', showing where money is being placed. Remember: you invest IN something, putting your money into it.

wrong: He specializes on marketing.
right: He specializes in marketing.
why: 'Specialize' pairs with 'in', describing a particular area of expertise. This follows the same pattern as 'interested in' and 'invest in' - many expertise-related verbs use 'in'.

wrong: I rely with my colleagues.
right: I rely on my colleagues.
why: 'Rely' pairs with 'on', just like 'depend on' - the two words are near-synonyms and share the same preposition. If you remember 'depend on', you'll automatically remember 'rely on' too.

wrong: She consists in three parts.
right: It consists of three parts.
why: 'Consist' pairs with 'of', describing what something is made up of. This is a simple fixed phrase: consist OF + [the parts].

wrong: He is engaged with her.
right: He is engaged to her.
why: Just like 'married to', the expression 'engaged to' describes a romantic commitment, and it never takes 'with'. Group 'engaged to' and 'married to' together since they share the same pattern.

wrong: I subscribed for the newsletter.
right: I subscribed to the newsletter.
why: 'Subscribe' pairs with 'to', showing what service or publication you're signing up for. Remember the phrase as a whole: subscribe TO.

wrong: She contributed on the project.
right: She contributed to the project.
why: 'Contribute' pairs with 'to', describing what something is added toward. This matches the pattern of many verbs showing direction or addition, like 'add to' and 'lead to'.

wrong: He is qualified in this job.
right: He is qualified for this job.
why: 'Qualified' pairs with 'for', showing the role or position someone is suited for. Practice this as one phrase: qualified FOR a job/role.

wrong: I dreamed with becoming a doctor.
right: I dreamed of becoming a doctor.
why: 'Dream' pairs with 'of' plus the -ing form when describing an ambition or wish. Remember the complete pattern: dream OF doing something.

wrong: She was disappointed of the results.
right: She was disappointed with the results.
why: 'Disappointed' usually pairs with 'with' (or sometimes 'by'), but never 'of'. When talking about being let down by something, default to 'disappointed with'.

wrong: Me and him went to the market.
right: He and I went to the market.
why: When a pronoun is the SUBJECT of a sentence (the one doing the action), you must use subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they), not object pronouns (me, him, her). A simple trick: remove the other person from the sentence and see which sounds right alone - 'Me went to the market' sounds wrong, so you know 'I' is correct.

wrong: Him and me are friends.
right: He and I are friends.
why: Just like the previous example, both pronouns acting as the subject need the subject form. Test each pronoun alone: 'Him is my friend' sounds wrong, telling you it should be 'he'.

wrong: This is a gift for you and I.
right: This is a gift for you and me.
why: After a preposition like 'for', 'to', or 'with', you always need the OBJECT form of the pronoun (me, him, her), not the subject form. Test it alone: 'This is a gift for I' sounds wrong, so 'me' is correct.

wrong: Between you and I, this is a secret.
right: Between you and me, this is a secret.
why: 'Between' is a preposition, so it must be followed by object pronouns, just like 'for' and 'with'. This is one of the most common mistakes even among confident English speakers, so it's worth memorizing this exact phrase: 'between you and me'.

wrong: Her and me are going shopping.
right: She and I are going shopping.
why: Both pronouns here are acting as the subject of 'are going', so they need the subject forms (she, I), not object forms. Always test pronouns separately: 'Her is going shopping' clearly sounds wrong.

wrong: I gave the book to she.
right: I gave the book to her.
why: After the preposition 'to', you need the object pronoun 'her', not the subject pronoun 'she'. Remember: subject pronouns do the action, object pronouns receive it or follow a preposition.

wrong: Give the pen to they.
right: Give the pen to them.
why: Just like 'her', after a preposition ('to'), 'they' must change into its object form, 'them'. Practice the full set of object pronouns: me, you, him, her, it, us, them.

wrong: This is between he and I.
right: This is between him and me.
why: Both pronouns here follow the preposition 'between', so both need to be in the object form. Test each one alone: 'between he' and 'between I' both sound wrong, confirming 'him' and 'me' are correct.

wrong: My friend and me went to the movie.
right: My friend and I went to the movie.
why: Since 'my friend and I' together form the subject of 'went', the pronoun must be in subject form. A reliable trick: drop 'my friend' temporarily - 'Me went to the movie' sounds wrong, confirming 'I' is correct.

wrong: Us students need more time.
right: We students need more time.
why: When a pronoun comes directly before a noun acting as the subject (students), it must be in subject form. Remove the noun to test: 'Us need more time' sounds wrong, so 'we' is correct.

wrong: Let we go to the party.
right: Let us go to the party.
why: After the verb 'let', you always need the object pronoun form, not the subject form. This is a fixed expression - 'let us' (or 'let's') - that's worth memorizing directly.

wrong: Him is my brother.
right: He is my brother.
why: As the subject of the sentence, this pronoun must take the subject form 'he', not the object form 'him'. This is a very basic but common mistake, especially among absolute beginners.

wrong: Me want to go home.
right: I want to go home.
why: As the subject performing the action 'want', this pronoun must be 'I', the subject form, not 'me'. This exact mistake is extremely common among very early learners, so it's worth practicing simple sentences like this repeatedly.

wrong: This book belongs to I.
right: This book belongs to me.
why: After the preposition 'to', object pronouns are required, so 'me' is correct here, not 'I'. Whenever a preposition comes right before a pronoun, automatically switch to the object form.

wrong: He is more clever than me is.
right: He is more clever than I am.
why: This sentence mixes forms incorrectly - when there's a verb after 'than' (like 'am'), you need the subject pronoun 'I', not the object pronoun 'me'. If no verb follows 'than', 'than me' is fine in casual speech, but with a verb, use the subject form.

wrong: Who's coat is this?
right: Whose coat is this?
why: "Who's" is a contraction meaning 'who is' or 'who has', while 'whose' shows possession (asking who owns something) - the two words sound identical but mean completely different things. Whenever you're asking about ownership, use 'whose', never "who's".

wrong: Its a beautiful day.
right: It's a beautiful day.
why: "It's" (with an apostrophe) is short for 'it is' or 'it has', while 'its' (no apostrophe) shows possession, like 'the dog wagged its tail'. A simple test: try expanding it to 'it is' - if that makes sense, use "it's".

wrong: The dog wagged it's tail.
right: The dog wagged its tail.
why: This is the reverse mistake - here, 'its' shows that the tail belongs to the dog, so no apostrophe is needed. Test again: 'the dog wagged it is tail' doesn't make sense, confirming you need the possessive 'its', without an apostrophe.

wrong: This is their's house.
right: This is their house. / This house is theirs.
why: Possessive pronouns like 'theirs', 'yours', 'hers', and 'ours' never take an apostrophe - only the possessive adjective form ('their') goes directly before a noun, while 'theirs' stands alone. Remember: no possessive pronoun in English ever uses an apostrophe.

wrong: Is this book your's?
right: Is this book yours?
why: Just like 'theirs', the possessive pronoun 'yours' never takes an apostrophe. Memorize this list without apostrophes: mine, yours, his, hers, its, ours, theirs.

wrong: That is her's bag.
right: That is her bag. / That bag is hers.
why: 'Her' is used directly before a noun (her bag), while 'hers' stands alone without a noun following it (that bag is hers) - neither version ever takes an apostrophe. Choose the right form based on whether a noun comes right after it.

wrong: I saw hisself in the mirror.
right: I saw himself in the mirror.
why: The reflexive pronoun for 'he' is 'himself', not 'hisself' - this is a common but nonstandard form. Memorize the correct reflexive set: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves.

wrong: They hurt theirself while playing.
right: They hurt themselves while playing.
why: The reflexive pronoun for 'they' is 'themselves' (plural), not 'theirself', which isn't a standard English word at all. Always use 'themselves' for a plural subject reflecting the action back on itself.

wrong: I did it by myself alone.
right: I did it by myself.
why: This isn't wrong grammatically, but it's redundant - 'by myself' already means 'alone', so adding both words together repeats the same meaning unnecessarily. Choose either 'by myself' or 'alone', not both together.

wrong: Everybody should bring their own book, isn't it?
right: Everybody should bring their own book, shouldn't they?
why: 'Their' is commonly used as a gender-neutral singular pronoun referring back to 'everybody' in modern English, but the question tag must match the actual subject and verb ('shouldn't they'), not a generic 'isn't it'. Always build your tag question from the real subject and verb of the main sentence, not a fixed phrase.

wrong: Each student must submit his assignment, isn't it?
right: Each student must submit their assignment, shouldn't they?
why: Two fixes are needed here: modern English commonly uses 'their' instead of assuming 'his' for a general, unspecified student, and the question tag must match the actual meaning ('shouldn't they', not 'isn't it', which doesn't relate to the sentence at all). Match your tag question to the actual subject and verb of the main sentence.

wrong: The company announced their new product.
right: The company announced its new product.
why: When 'company' refers to a single business entity (not people), it takes the singular pronoun 'its', not 'their' - though in British English, companies are sometimes treated as plural (they/their), especially referring to the people within them. For general clarity, 'its' is the safer, more standard choice in American English.

wrong: Everyone has their own opinions, and he should respect that.
right: Everyone has their own opinions, and they should respect that.
why: Once you start referring to 'everyone' with 'their' (a very common and now widely accepted choice), stay consistent and continue using 'they/their' throughout, rather than switching to 'he'. Consistency in pronoun choice throughout a sentence or paragraph is key.

wrong: Me myself did this work alone.
right: I myself did this work alone.
why: When adding emphasis with a reflexive pronoun (myself, himself), the main pronoun before it must still be in subject form if it's the subject of the sentence. Keep 'I myself', not 'me myself', when emphasizing who performed an action.

wrong: The teacher gave homework to we students.
right: The teacher gave homework to us students.
why: After the preposition 'to', the pronoun needs to be in object form, even when followed directly by a noun (students). Test it alone: 'gave homework to we' sounds wrong, confirming 'us' is correct.

wrong: I like they very much.
right: I like them very much.
why: As the object of the verb 'like' (receiving the action), the pronoun must be in object form, 'them', not the subject form 'they'. Whenever a pronoun comes right after a verb as its object, use the object form.

wrong: She invited he to the party.
right: She invited him to the party.
why: Just like 'them', the pronoun here is the object of 'invited', so it needs the object form 'him', not the subject form 'he'. This same pattern applies to every pronoun receiving an action.

wrong: I will call she tomorrow.
right: I will call her tomorrow.
why: As the object of 'call', this pronoun needs the object form 'her', not the subject form 'she'. Keep practicing this pattern with different verbs (call, invite, see, help) plus object pronouns.

wrong: This problem is between they and us.
right: This problem is between them and us.
why: As before, the preposition 'between' requires object pronouns on both sides - 'them' and 'us', not 'they' and 'we'. Always check both pronouns individually when they're joined by 'and' after a preposition.

wrong: I and my brother are going out.
right: My brother and I are going out.
why: While not strictly incorrect grammatically, it's considered more polite and standard in English to mention yourself last when listing people together. Rearrange the order so 'I' comes at the end: 'My brother and I', not 'I and my brother'.

wrong: Me and my friends are planning a trip.
right: My friends and I are planning a trip.
why: Two things to fix here: use the subject pronoun 'I' since it's the subject of 'are planning', and place yourself last in the list, after 'my friends'. Combine both rules together for a natural, correct sentence.

wrong: This decision affects you and I equally.
right: This decision affects you and me equally.
why: As the object of 'affects' (receiving the action), the pronoun needs the object form 'me', not 'I'. This exact phrase - 'you and I' vs 'you and me' - is one of the most commonly confused pairs in English, even among native speakers.

wrong: The manager spoke to Sarah and I yesterday.
right: The manager spoke to Sarah and me yesterday.
why: After the preposition 'to', both nouns and pronouns need the object form, so 'me' is correct, not 'I'. A quick trick: remove 'Sarah and' temporarily - 'spoke to I' clearly sounds wrong, confirming 'me' is correct.

wrong: Just between you and I, I don't trust him.
right: Just between you and me, I don't trust him.
why: This repeats the very common 'between you and I' mistake covered earlier - since 'between' is a preposition, it always needs object pronouns. This phrase is worth memorizing completely correctly since it appears so often in daily conversation.

wrong: He is the one whom I believe did it.
right: He is the one who I believe did it.
why: 'Who' is correct here because it's the subject of 'did it', even with 'I believe' inserted in between - inserted phrases like 'I believe' or 'I think' don't change which pronoun form is needed. Ignore short inserted phrases like 'I think' or 'I believe' when deciding between who and whom - focus on the actual verb the pronoun connects to.

wrong: Whom is at the door?
right: Who is at the door?
why: Use 'who' when the pronoun is the SUBJECT of the sentence (the one doing the action, here 'being at the door'), and reserve 'whom' for when the pronoun is the object. A simple trick: if you could answer with 'he/she' (subject form), use 'who'; if you'd answer with 'him/her' (object form), use 'whom'.

wrong: Everyone lost their keys, but nobody knows who's it belongs to.
right: Everyone lost their keys, but nobody knows whose they belong to.
why: This combines two mistakes: 'whose' (possession) is needed instead of "who's" (who is), and the verb must agree with the plural 'keys' ('belong', not 'belongs'). Double-check both the pronoun spelling and the verb agreement in longer sentences like this.

wrong: I need to find out who's bag this is.
right: I need to find out whose bag this is.
why: Since this question asks about ownership (who does this bag belong to), it needs 'whose', not the contraction "who's" (who is). Repeat this rule until it's automatic: ownership question = whose, contraction of 'who is/has' = who's.

wrong: She is the woman who's car broke down.
right: She is the woman whose car broke down.
why: Again, this describes ownership (the woman's car), so it needs 'whose', never "who's". This specific mistake - confusing whose and who's - is one of the most common spelling mix-ups even among fluent English speakers.

wrong: This is the house who's roof is damaged.
right: This is the house whose roof is damaged.
why: 'Whose' can also be used for objects and things, not just people, when showing possession (the house's roof). Remember: 'whose' isn't only for people - it works for anything that 'owns' or 'has' something.

wrong: I don't know who's turn it is next.
right: I don't know whose turn it is next.
why: This is once more the ownership meaning ('whose turn', meaning which person's turn), so 'whose' is correct, not the contraction "who's". After all these examples, the rule should now feel automatic: possession = whose, contraction = who's.

wrong: You are going where?
right: Where are you going?
why: English questions starting with a question word (where, what, when, why) need that word FIRST, followed by the helping verb, then the subject - this is called inversion. The pattern is: [question word] + [helping verb] + [subject] + [rest of sentence].

wrong: What you are doing?
right: What are you doing?
why: After the question word 'what', the helping verb (are) must come before the subject (you), not after it. Always invert: question word + verb + subject, never question word + subject + verb.

wrong: What means this word?
right: What does this word mean?
why: This sentence needs 'does' inserted before the subject, and the main verb ('mean') must return to its base form at the end. Remember the full structure: question word + does + subject + base verb.

wrong: How I can improve my English?
right: How can I improve my English?
why: With modal verbs like 'can', the modal must come before the subject in a question, not after it. The pattern is: question word + modal (can/will/should) + subject + base verb.

wrong: Why you didn't call me?
right: Why didn't you call me?
why: The negative helping verb 'didn't' must come before the subject 'you' in a question, following the same inversion rule as positive questions. Keep the pattern consistent even in negative questions: question word + didn't/doesn't/don't + subject + base verb.

wrong: When will you will arrive?
right: When will you arrive?
why: This sentence repeats 'will' twice, which is unnecessary - a question only needs one helping verb. Remove the duplicate and keep just one 'will' before the subject.

wrong: Who is knows the answer?
right: Who knows the answer?
why: When 'who' is the SUBJECT of the question (asking who performs the action), no extra helping verb like 'is' or 'does' is needed - simply add 's' to the main verb as usual. This is a special exception: when 'who' or 'what' asks about the subject itself, use normal statement word order.

wrong: What does happen if I click this?
right: What happens if I click this?
why: Similar to the 'who' example, when 'what' is asking about the subject performing an action, you don't need 'does' - just use the regular verb form with 's'. Test this by checking if the question word itself is doing the action - if so, skip 'do/does/did'.

wrong: Do you can speak French?
right: Can you speak French?
why: Modal verbs (can, will, should, must) never need 'do/does/did' added before them - the modal itself moves to the front of the question. Remove 'do' entirely whenever the sentence already has a modal verb.

wrong: Does he can drive?
right: Can he drive?
why: Just like the previous example, 'does' should never be combined with a modal verb like 'can' - the modal alone inverts with the subject. Keep this rule consistent: modals handle their own question formation without 'do/does/did'.

wrong: Is you happy?
right: Are you happy?
why: The helping verb 'be' must match the subject in questions too, not just statements - 'you' needs 'are', not 'is'. Always check subject-verb agreement even when forming questions with 'be'.

wrong: What time is it now, isn't it?
right: What time is it now?
why: This sentence mixes a genuine information question with a question tag, which doesn't belong here - question tags are only added to statements, not to questions that already ask for information. Remove the tag entirely from a direct question like this.

wrong: You like coffee, do you?
right: You like coffee, don't you?
why: In a question tag, when the main sentence is positive ('you like coffee'), the tag must be negative ('don't you'), not positive. Remember: positive statement + negative tag, negative statement + positive tag.

wrong: She isn't here, is she isn't?
right: She isn't here, is she?
why: This sentence repeats the negative unnecessarily - the tag only needs 'is she', not 'is she isn't', which duplicates the negation. Keep tags short: just the helping verb and subject pronoun, nothing more.

wrong: How old are you having?
right: How old are you?
why: Age in English is expressed with 'be' (are you, is he), not with 'have' - many languages use a 'have' structure for age, but English doesn't. Memorize this fixed pattern: 'how old are you', never 'how old do you have' or similar.

wrong: What is your name is?
right: What is your name?
why: This sentence repeats 'is' unnecessarily - the question only needs it once. Keep questions with 'be' simple: question word + is/are + subject, no repetition.

wrong: Whom is calling, please?
right: Who is calling, please?
why: Since the caller is the SUBJECT of this question (performing the action of calling), 'who' is correct here, not 'whom' (which is reserved for objects). Use 'who' whenever the question word is the one doing the action.

wrong: How much money do you have it?
right: How much money do you have?
why: This sentence has an extra, unnecessary 'it' at the end - once you've asked about 'how much money', there's no need for an additional pronoun repeating the object. Remove the redundant 'it' since 'money' was already specified in the question.

wrong: Can you tell me what time is it?
right: Can you tell me what time it is?
why: This is an embedded question - once a question is placed inside a larger sentence, the normal question inversion disappears, and it returns to statement order (subject before verb). Remember: embedded questions (inside 'can you tell me...', 'I wonder...', 'do you know...') always use statement word order.

wrong: Do you know where does she live?
right: Do you know where she lives?
why: Just like the previous example, this is an embedded question, so 'does' should be removed and the verb should take the 's' ending directly (lives). Practice converting direct questions into embedded ones: remove do/does/did and adjust the main verb.

wrong: I wonder what is he thinking.
right: I wonder what he is thinking.
why: Since this is an embedded question after 'I wonder', it needs statement word order (subject before verb: he is), not question order (is he). This exact type of mistake is very common when converting a curious thought into a full sentence.

wrong: What time does the movie starts?
right: What time does the movie start?
why: Once 'does' is used in the question, the main verb must return to its base form ('start'), not keep the 's' ending ('starts'). Remember: 'does' already carries the third-person 's', so the main verb stays plain.

wrong: Why did you went there?
right: Why did you go there?
why: Since 'did' already shows the question is in the past tense, the main verb after it must stay in its base form ('go'), not the past tense ('went'). This is one of the most frequent question mistakes, so it's worth practicing repeatedly.

wrong: Where did you bought this?
right: Where did you buy this?
why: Just like the previous example, 'did' requires the base form of the verb afterward ('buy'), not the past tense form ('bought'). Keep this rule firmly in mind: did + base verb, never did + past tense verb.

wrong: What did happen yesterday?
right: What happened yesterday?
why: Similar to the 'who' and 'what' subject-question exception covered earlier, when 'what' is asking about the subject of the action, no 'did' is needed - simply use the past tense verb directly. Test it: is 'what' asking about who/what did something? If yes, skip 'did' entirely.

wrong: Do you have ever been to Paris?
right: Have you ever been to Paris?
why: This question uses present perfect tense ('have been'), which doesn't need 'do' added - the helping verb 'have' itself moves to the front of the question. Remove 'do' whenever the sentence already has 'have/has' as its helping verb.

wrong: Did you have finished your homework?
right: Have you finished your homework?
why: Similar to the previous example, present perfect questions use 'have/has' at the front, not 'did'. Never combine 'did' with 'have' in the same question - choose the correct tense structure first.

wrong: Whose is this book?
right: Whose book is this?
why: 'Whose' should be placed directly before the noun it's asking about (book), not separated from it by 'is'. Keep 'whose' and the noun together: whose + noun + is + this/that.

wrong: How long you have been waiting?
right: How long have you been waiting?
why: In this present perfect continuous question, 'have' must come before the subject (you), following the normal inversion pattern. Keep the helping verb order consistent: question word + have/has + subject + been + verb-ing.

wrong: Why is that you're upset?
right: Why are you upset?
why: This sentence unnecessarily complicates a simple question with extra words ('is that') that aren't needed - just ask directly. Simplify by removing the extra 'is that' phrase entirely.

wrong: Could you tell me what is the time?
right: Could you tell me what the time is?
why: Since this is an embedded question after 'could you tell me', it needs statement word order (the time is), not question order (is the time). This is the same embedded question rule appearing once more in a polite request.

wrong: You don't like it, do you don't?
right: You don't like it, do you?
why: This tag repeats the negative unnecessarily - since the main sentence is already negative ('don't like'), the tag just needs the positive form 'do you', without repeating 'don't'. Keep tags short and simple: just helping verb + subject pronoun.

wrong: Where is it that you work?
right: Where do you work?
why: This sentence uses an unnecessarily complicated structure ('is it that') when a simple direct question works better. Simplify to the standard question pattern: question word + do/does + subject + verb.

wrong: What for did you do that?
right: Why did you do that? / What did you do that for?
why: 'What for' isn't placed correctly at the start of this question - either replace it entirely with 'why', or move 'for' to the end of the sentence in casual speech. Choose one of the two natural, correct structures instead of this mixed-up version.

wrong: Have you ever went to Japan?
right: Have you ever been to Japan?
why: After 'have', questions need the past participle form of the verb ('been'), not the simple past tense form ('went'). Remember: 'have/has' always needs the past participle afterward, in questions just as in statements.

wrong: Since when you are learning English?
right: Since when have you been learning English?
why: This describes an action continuing from the past to now, so it needs present perfect continuous ('have been learning'), and 'have' must come before the subject. Combine both rules: correct tense (have been + verb-ing) and correct question order (have before subject).

wrong: What is it what you want?
right: What is it that you want?
why: This sentence incorrectly repeats 'what' - the second part should use 'that', not 'what' again, to connect the clarifying clause. Whenever you're rephrasing a question for emphasis, use 'that' to connect the two parts, not a repeated question word.

wrong: Amn't I invited to the party?
right: Aren't I invited to the party?
why: Although 'I am' would logically seem to need 'amn't' as its question form, that word isn't standard English - the accepted irregular form for 'I am not' in a question is 'aren't I', not 'amn't I' or 'ain't I'. This is a quirky exception worth memorizing on its own, since it doesn't follow the usual pattern.

wrong: How do you spell it that word?
right: How do you spell that word?
why: This sentence has both 'it' and 'that word' referring to the same thing, which is redundant - only one reference to the word is needed. Remove the extra pronoun 'it' since 'that word' already identifies what you're asking about.

wrong: What happened to did you?
right: What happened to you?
why: This sentence incorrectly adds 'did' into a question where 'what' is already the subject performing the action (happened) - no extra helping verb is needed. Remember the subject-question exception: when 'what' asks about the subject, skip do/does/did entirely.

wrong: Who you are?
right: Who are you?
why: Even though 'who' starts the question, the helping verb 'are' must still come before the subject 'you', following normal inversion rules. This mistake often happens because 'who' feels like it should behave differently, but it follows the same inversion pattern as other question words when it's not the subject.

wrong: Why do you not like it?
right: Why don't you like it?
why: While 'why do you not like it' is technically grammatical, the much more natural and common form contracts 'do not' into 'don't' in this question pattern. In everyday spoken and written English, always prefer the contracted form 'don't' in negative questions like this.

wrong: What do you think about it think?
right: What do you think about it?
why: This sentence mistakenly repeats the word 'think' at the end - the question only needs it once, right after 'do you'. Always check your questions for accidentally repeated words, especially in longer sentences.

wrong: Isn't it, that this is correct?
right: Isn't this correct? / This is correct, isn't it?
why: This sentence awkwardly combines a tag question format with unnecessary extra words - choose either a clean direct question ('Isn't this correct?') or a proper statement-plus-tag structure ('This is correct, isn't it?'). Avoid mixing partial structures together into one confusing sentence.

wrong: He said that he is tired.
right: He said that he was tired.
why: When the reporting verb ('said') is in the past tense, the tense inside the reported clause usually shifts back one step too - present simple ('is') becomes past simple ('was'). This shift is called 'backshifting', and it applies to almost all reported statements about the past.

wrong: She said that she is going to the market.
right: She said that she was going to the market.
why: Just like the previous example, present continuous ('is going') shifts back to past continuous ('was going') when reporting what someone said in the past. Keep applying this same backshift rule consistently across all present tenses.

wrong: He told me that he will help me.
right: He told me that he would help me.
why: The future tense marker 'will' shifts back to 'would' in reported speech, following the same backshifting pattern as other tenses. Remember this specific shift: will becomes would when reporting past speech.

wrong: She said that she can swim well.
right: She said that she could swim well.
why: The modal verb 'can' shifts back to 'could' in reported speech, just as other tenses shift back one step. Memorize this modal shift alongside will/would: can becomes could.

wrong: He said that he has finished the work.
right: He said that he had finished the work.
why: Present perfect ('has finished') shifts back to past perfect ('had finished') in reported speech. This follows the same backshifting logic - present perfect moves back to past perfect, just as present simple moves to past simple.

wrong: She said that she went there yesterday.
right: She said that she had gone there the day before.
why: This has two fixes: past simple ('went') often shifts back to past perfect ('had gone') in reported speech, and the specific time word 'yesterday' must change to 'the day before' since we're no longer speaking on that same day. Whenever reporting speech from a different day, adjust both the tense and any time expressions.

wrong: He asked me did I like the movie.
right: He asked me if I liked the movie.
why: Reported yes/no questions need 'if' or 'whether' added, and the word order returns to normal statement order (no more question inversion). Never use direct question word order (did I) inside reported speech - always convert to statement order with if/whether.

wrong: She asked me where do I live.
right: She asked me where I lived.
why: Just like yes/no questions, reported WH-questions (where, what, why) also drop the question word order and inversion, returning to normal statement order. Remove 'do/does/did' entirely and shift the tense onto the main verb.

wrong: She told me that she is coming today.
right: She told me that she was coming that day.
why: This combines two shifts: 'is coming' becomes 'was coming' (backshift), and 'today' becomes 'that day' since the reporting happens at a different time than the original statement. Whenever reporting speech later, adjust time words that refer to 'now' into words that refer to 'then'.

wrong: He said that he will come here.
right: He said that he would come there.
why: Besides 'will' shifting to 'would', the place word 'here' must shift to 'there' since the reporter is typically not in the same location as the original speaker was referring to. Remember this place shift alongside the tense shift: here becomes there in reported speech.

wrong: She said she must leave immediately.
right: She said she had to leave immediately. / She said she must leave immediately (both acceptable).
why: 'Must' (meaning obligation) can shift to 'had to' in reported speech, though 'must' can also stay unchanged in many cases, especially for strong obligation or rules. This modal is a partial exception - it has some flexibility in reported speech, unlike 'will' and 'can', which always shift.

wrong: She said that this is her book.
right: She said that was her book. / She said that this was her book.
why: Beyond the tense shift (is to was), the demonstrative pronoun 'this' often shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something not physically present at the time of reporting. Consider shifting 'this/these' to 'that/those' when reporting speech at a later time or in a different place.

wrong: She asked me can I help her.
right: She asked me if I could help her.
why: This combines two fixes: add 'if' since this is a yes/no question, and shift 'can' back to 'could' following the standard modal backshift rule. Both fixes (adding if/whether AND backshifting the modal) are usually needed together in reported yes/no questions.

wrong: He told me that he doesn't like coffee.
right: He told me that he didn't like coffee.
why: Present simple negative ('doesn't like') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't like') in reported speech, following the same backshift rule as positive sentences. Negative sentences follow exactly the same backshifting pattern as positive ones - don't forget to shift these too.

wrong: She said that she isn't coming to the party.
right: She said that she wasn't coming to the party.
why: Just like the previous example, present continuous negative ('isn't coming') shifts to past continuous negative ('wasn't coming'). Keep applying the backshift rule consistently to every verb form you encounter, whether positive or negative.

wrong: He said he has never been to Paris.
right: He said he had never been to Paris.
why: Present perfect ('has been') shifts to past perfect ('had been') in reported speech, even with 'never' inserted in the middle. The presence of 'never' or other adverbs doesn't change the backshift rule - it still applies to the main verb tense.

wrong: She told me she is going to study medicine.
right: She told me she was going to study medicine.
why: The 'going to' future form shifts back to its past tense version ('was going to') in reported speech, just like other present tenses. Remember that 'going to' future follows the same backshift pattern as present continuous, since it uses the same verb structure.

wrong: He asked where I am going.
right: He asked where I was going.
why: Even in this embedded question (already in normal statement order), the tense still needs to backshift from present ('am going') to past ('was going'). Don't forget the tense shift just because the question word order was already correct - both fixes are usually needed together.

wrong: He told me I should to call him back.
right: He told me I should call him back.
why: This is a separate mistake unrelated to backshifting - 'should' is a modal verb, and modal verbs are never followed by 'to' before the base verb. Remove 'to' after any modal verb (should, can, will, must), whether in reported speech or a direct statement.

wrong: She asked if I was here yesterday.
right: She asked if I had been there the day before.
why: This needs several shifts together: 'was' becomes 'had been' (past shifts to past perfect when reporting an even earlier past event), 'here' becomes 'there', and 'yesterday' becomes 'the day before'. Complex reported speech often requires multiple simultaneous shifts - practice identifying all of them in one sentence.

wrong: He said that he would came tomorrow.
right: He said that he would come the next day.
why: This has two issues: after 'would', the verb must stay in base form ('come'), not past tense ('came'), and 'tomorrow' should shift to 'the next day'. Always check the verb form after modals like would/could/should - they need the base verb, never a past tense form.

wrong: She told me that she needs my help.
right: She told me that she needed my help.
why: Present simple ('needs') shifts back to past simple ('needed') in reported speech, following the standard backshift rule. This is a simple, regular example of the core backshift rule applying to an ordinary present simple verb.

wrong: He asked me what I am doing here.
right: He asked me what I was doing there.
why: This needs two shifts: the tense backshifts from present continuous ('am doing') to past continuous ('was doing'), and the place word shifts from 'here' to 'there'. Combine the tense shift and place shift together whenever both apply in the same sentence.

wrong: She said that tomorrow she will visit her grandmother.
right: She said that the next day she would visit her grandmother.
why: This needs both the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day') and the modal shift ('will' to 'would'), applied together in the same sentence. Whenever a sentence has both a time word and a future tense marker, remember to shift both.

wrong: He told me he can't come today.
right: He told me he couldn't come that day.
why: This needs both the modal shift ('can't' to 'couldn't') and the time word shift ('today' to 'that day'), combined in one sentence. Practice looking for multiple elements needing change within a single reported sentence - modals, time words, and place words often appear together.

wrong: She asked whether I have finished my work.
right: She asked whether I had finished my work.
why: Even though 'whether' correctly introduces this reported yes/no question, the tense still needs to shift from present perfect ('have finished') to past perfect ('had finished'). Using 'whether' or 'if' correctly doesn't exempt you from also applying the tense backshift.

wrong: He said he is having lunch right now.
right: He said he was having lunch at that time.
why: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('right now' to 'at that time'), since 'right now' only makes sense from the original speaker's perspective at the moment of speaking. Time phrases describing the present moment ('now', 'right now', 'currently') typically shift to phrases describing a past moment when reporting later.

wrong: She told me that her sister lives here.
right: She told me that her sister lived there.
why: Present simple ('lives') shifts to past simple ('lived'), and 'here' shifts to 'there' since the reporter is describing a location relative to the original speaker's perspective. Whenever a place word appears in reported speech, consider whether it needs to shift along with the tense.

wrong: He asked if I will be free next week.
right: He asked if I would be free the following week.
why: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the time phrase shift ('next week' to 'the following week'). Always check for both a tense/modal shift and a time-word shift together whenever future time references appear in reported speech.

wrong: He told me he doesn't want to go there anymore.
right: He told me he didn't want to go there anymore.
why: Present simple negative ('doesn't want') shifts back to past simple negative ('didn't want'), following the standard rule even with the word 'anymore' included. Additional words like 'anymore' or 'still' don't change the basic backshift rule for the main verb.

wrong: She asked me if I am ready to leave.
right: She asked me if I was ready to leave.
why: Even with 'if' correctly used for this yes/no question, the verb 'am' still needs to shift back to 'was' in reported speech. Using the correct question-reporting word ('if') doesn't replace the need for tense backshifting.

wrong: He said that he was working on it and he'll finish soon.
right: He said that he was working on it and that he would finish soon.
why: The second half of this sentence still needs the future shift from 'will' to 'would', even though the first half was already correctly shifted. When a sentence has multiple clauses being reported, check that EACH one has the necessary tense shifts, not just the first.

wrong: She told me that she is thinking about moving abroad.
right: She told me that she was thinking about moving abroad.
why: Present continuous ('is thinking') shifts to past continuous ('was thinking'), following the standard backshift pattern once more. This is a straightforward, regular application of the core backshift rule for present continuous tense.

wrong: He asked me what time does the store close.
right: He asked me what time the store closed.
why: This needs two fixes: remove 'does' since this is now an embedded/reported question with normal word order, and shift the tense onto the main verb ('closed') directly. Combine the word-order fix (statement order, no do/does/did) with the tense backshift for a fully correct reported question.

wrong: She said she has been feeling unwell lately.
right: She said she had been feeling unwell lately.
why: Present perfect continuous ('has been feeling') shifts to past perfect continuous ('had been feeling') in reported speech. This shows the backshift rule extending to the more complex continuous perfect tenses as well, not just simple tenses.

wrong: He told me he will always remember this day.
right: He told me he would always remember that day.
why: This needs both the modal shift ('will' to 'would') and the demonstrative shift ('this day' to 'that day'), applied together. Whenever 'this' appears alongside a future tense marker, expect to shift both elements when reporting.

wrong: She asked if I can meet her tomorrow.
right: She asked if I could meet her the next day.
why: This combines the modal shift ('can' to 'could') with the time word shift ('tomorrow' to 'the next day'), both required together. This exact combination - modal plus time word - appears very frequently in reported speech exercises.

wrong: He said that he is planning to visit next month.
right: He said that he was planning to visit the following month.
why: This needs both the tense shift (present continuous to past continuous) and the time phrase shift ('next month' to 'the following month'). Keep practicing this combination until identifying both necessary shifts becomes automatic.

wrong: She told me she doesn't know the answer.
right: She told me she didn't know the answer.
why: This is a simple, direct application of the core backshift rule: present simple negative shifts to past simple negative. Practicing simple, clear examples like this one helps reinforce the fundamental rule before tackling more complex combined shifts.

wrong: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting today.
right: He asked whether I was coming to the meeting that day.
why: Even though the tense ('was coming') is already correctly in past continuous, the time word 'today' still needs to shift to 'that day' since we're reporting this at a different time. Don't forget to check time words even when the tense itself is already correctly shifted.

wrong: She said that she can't believe the news.
right: She said that she couldn't believe the news.
why: This is a direct, simple application of the modal backshift rule: can't shifts to couldn't. This example reinforces the modal-shift rule in its most straightforward form.

wrong: He told me he is not sure about the plan.
right: He told me he was not sure about the plan.
why: Present simple with 'be' ('is not sure') shifts to past simple ('was not sure'), following the same rule as all other present tense forms. This shows the backshift rule applying even to simple descriptive statements using 'be', not just action verbs.

wrong: She asked if I have any questions.
right: She asked if I had any questions.
why: Present simple ('have') shifts to past simple ('had') in this reported yes/no question, combining the 'if' structure with the standard tense backshift. This is one of the most common reported speech patterns in everyday conversation, especially in meetings and interviews.

wrong: He said that he was here now.
right: He said that he was there then.
why: Both 'here' and 'now' need to shift to 'there' and 'then' respectively, since the reporter isn't speaking from the same place or moment as the original speaker. Whenever both a place word and a time word appear together in the original quote, shift both consistently.

wrong: She told me that this was the best day of her life.
right: She told me that that was the best day of her life.
why: The demonstrative 'this' shifts to 'that' in reported speech when referring to something no longer immediate or present. Practice shifting demonstratives (this/these to that/those) alongside tense shifts whenever they appear in the original statement.

wrong: I went to the store I bought some milk.
right: I went to the store, and I bought some milk.
why: These are two complete sentences (each has its own subject and verb) joined with no punctuation at all, which is called a run-on sentence. Fix it by adding a comma plus a joining word (and, but, so), or by using a period or semicolon to separate them into two sentences.

wrong: The movie was long it was very boring.
right: The movie was long, and it was very boring.
why: Two complete sentences here are joined with no punctuation at all - add a comma and 'and' since both ideas support each other (long AND boring). Remember: a comma alone can never join two full sentences; it always needs a joining word with it (unless using a semicolon).

wrong: We left early, we wanted to avoid traffic.
right: We left early because we wanted to avoid traffic. / We left early to avoid traffic.
why: Here, the second part explains the REASON for the first action, so 'because' (not just a comma) is the clearest connector. Whenever the second part explains 'why' the first part happened, use 'because' to join them properly.

wrong: Because I was tired.
right: I went to bed because I was tired.
why: This fragment starts with 'because', which makes it a dependent clause - it explains a reason but can't stand alone without a main sentence attached. Whenever a sentence begins with 'because', 'although', 'since', or 'when', make sure it's connected to a complete main clause.

wrong: Running through the park.
right: She was running through the park.
why: This fragment has no clear subject or complete verb - 'running' needs a helping verb (was/is/were) and a subject to become a full sentence. Always check that your sentence has both someone/something doing the action AND a complete verb form.

wrong: The book on the table.
right: The book on the table is mine.
why: This fragment only has a subject (the book) with a description (on the table), but no verb telling us what happens - it needs a verb to complete the thought. Ask yourself: what is happening, or what is being said about the subject? Add that missing verb.

wrong: Although she studied hard.
right: Although she studied hard, she didn't pass the exam.
why: Just like 'because', 'although' introduces a dependent clause that needs a main clause to complete the thought - it can't stand alone as a full sentence. Whenever you start with 'although' or 'even though', continue the sentence with what happened despite that condition.

wrong: When I arrived at the station.
right: When I arrived at the station, the train had already left.
why: This fragment describes a time ('when I arrived') but doesn't tell us what happened at that time - it needs a main clause attached. Time clauses starting with 'when', 'while', or 'as soon as' always need to be connected to a complete sentence.

wrong: A very talented singer.
right: She is a very talented singer.
why: This fragment describes something but has no subject or verb showing who or what it's describing. Add a subject (she, he, the artist) and a verb (is, was) to complete the sentence.

wrong: Working late every night.
right: He is working late every night.
why: Similar to the earlier example, this fragment has an action word ('working') but lacks a subject and helping verb to make it complete. Add 'he is', 'she was', or another subject plus helping verb before the action.

wrong: Which is why I left early.
right: I felt sick, which is why I left early.
why: This fragment begins with 'which', referring back to an idea that isn't stated - it needs the first part of the sentence to make sense. Whenever you start a sentence with 'which', check that the thing 'which' refers to is already mentioned.

wrong: Standing alone in the rain.
right: He was standing alone in the rain.
why: This fragment describes an action ('standing') without telling us who is doing it or providing a complete verb form. Add a subject and helping verb: 'he was standing', 'she is standing', to complete the thought.

wrong: Even though it was late.
right: Even though it was late, we decided to continue working.
why: Just like 'although', the phrase 'even though' introduces a condition that needs a main clause to complete the meaning. This dependent clause pattern is one of the most common causes of sentence fragments, so watch for these starting words carefully.

wrong: Since I have no money.
right: Since I have no money, I can't go shopping today.
why: 'Since' here introduces a reason, making this a dependent clause that needs a main clause attached to form a complete sentence. Whenever 'since' means 'because' at the start of a sentence, remember it needs a full second part.

wrong: The man wearing a blue shirt.
right: The man wearing a blue shirt is my uncle.
why: This fragment describes a person with detail (wearing a blue shirt) but never tells us what he does or what is said about him - a verb is missing. Complete the thought by adding a verb: is, was, walked, spoke, etc.

wrong: If you have time this weekend.
right: If you have time this weekend, let's meet for coffee.
why: 'If' introduces a condition, and conditions always need a result attached to become complete - this fragment is missing that result. Whenever you start with 'if', make sure to finish the thought with what would happen under that condition.

wrong: After finishing my homework.
right: After finishing my homework, I watched a movie.
why: 'After' introduces a time relationship, but this fragment doesn't say what happened after - it needs a main clause. Time words like 'after' and 'before' always need to connect to a complete action or event.

wrong: Before you leave the house.
right: Before you leave the house, please lock the door.
why: Just like 'after', 'before' sets up a time condition that needs to be finished with the actual instruction or event. This fragment leaves the reader wondering: before you leave, what should happen?

wrong: A beautiful sunset over the ocean.
right: We watched a beautiful sunset over the ocean.
why: This fragment is just a descriptive phrase with no subject performing an action or verb showing what happened. Add who is experiencing or doing something with this sunset, plus a verb.

wrong: While waiting for the bus.
right: While waiting for the bus, I read a book.
why: 'While' introduces a time relationship for something happening simultaneously, and this fragment needs the second event attached. Complete it by adding what you were doing during that waiting time.

wrong: Unless you apologize to her.
right: Unless you apologize to her, she won't forgive you.
why: 'Unless' sets up a condition (similar to 'if not'), and this fragment is missing the result of that condition. Always finish an 'unless' clause with what will or won't happen.

wrong: Trying his best to succeed.
right: He was trying his best to succeed.
why: This fragment has an action ('trying') but no clear subject and helping verb attached to make it a complete sentence. Add a subject and 'was/is' before the -ing verb to complete it.

wrong: Because of the heavy rain.
right: Because of the heavy rain, the match was postponed.
why: 'Because of' introduces a reason but this phrase alone doesn't tell us the RESULT of that reason - a main clause needs to follow. This is different from 'because' alone (a full clause) - 'because of' is followed by a noun phrase, then needs its own separate main clause.

wrong: Which made everyone very happy.
right: She won the competition, which made everyone very happy.
why: Starting a sentence with 'which' referring to something not yet mentioned leaves the reader confused about what 'which' refers to. Always attach this kind of clause to the sentence describing the event it refers to.

wrong: The girl who always arrives late.
right: The girl who always arrives late missed the meeting today.
why: This fragment describes a person in detail (who always arrives late) but never says what she actually did - the main verb for the sentence is missing. Complete the thought with what this person did or does.

wrong: In order to save money for college.
right: In order to save money for college, she took a part-time job.
why: 'In order to' introduces a purpose, but the fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it by stating the action someone took to achieve that goal.

wrong: As soon as the bell rings.
right: As soon as the bell rings, the students will leave the classroom.
why: 'As soon as' sets up a time condition, and this fragment is missing what happens at that moment. Always follow 'as soon as' with the resulting action.

wrong: Despite trying really hard.
right: Despite trying really hard, he didn't win the race.
why: 'Despite' introduces a contrast, and this fragment doesn't complete that contrast with what actually happened. Whenever you start with 'despite' or 'in spite of', finish with the surprising or contrasting outcome.

wrong: So that everyone could hear clearly.
right: She spoke loudly so that everyone could hear clearly.
why: 'So that' introduces a purpose, but this fragment doesn't tell us the action taken for that purpose. Complete the sentence with what was done to achieve this goal.

wrong: Considering how difficult the test was.
right: Considering how difficult the test was, she did very well.
why: 'Considering' introduces a factor to think about, but this fragment doesn't complete the thought about what happened given that factor. Add the main point or outcome you want to relate to this consideration.

wrong: The team that won the championship last year.
right: The team that won the championship last year is playing again tonight.
why: This fragment describes a team in detail but never says what the team is doing now - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing the team's current action or state.

wrong: Provided that you finish on time.
right: Provided that you finish on time, you can leave early.
why: 'Provided that' sets up a condition (similar to 'if'), and needs a result attached to complete the sentence. Always follow conditional phrases like this with what will happen if the condition is met.

wrong: Not knowing what to say.
right: Not knowing what to say, she stayed silent.
why: This fragment describes a state ('not knowing') but doesn't tell us what the person did as a result. Complete it by adding what action followed from that uncertainty.

wrong: Given the current circumstances.
right: Given the current circumstances, we need to postpone the event.
why: 'Given' here introduces a condition or context, but this fragment needs a main clause to state the actual decision or outcome. Complete the thought with what should be done considering this context.

wrong: Whatever you decide to do.
right: Whatever you decide to do, I will support you.
why: 'Whatever' introduces an open condition, and this fragment is missing the promise or statement connected to it. Complete it with what will happen regardless of the choice made.

wrong: The reason for her absence.
right: The reason for her absence was unclear.
why: This fragment names a topic (the reason) but doesn't say anything about it - a verb is needed to complete the thought. Add a verb like 'was', 'remains', or 'is' to finish the sentence.

wrong: To become a successful entrepreneur.
right: To become a successful entrepreneur, you need patience and hard work.
why: This fragment states a goal ('to become...') but doesn't say what's needed to achieve it - it's an incomplete infinitive phrase needing a main clause. Whenever a sentence starts with 'to + verb' describing a goal, complete it with what's required or what happens.

wrong: Regardless of the outcome.
right: Regardless of the outcome, we tried our best.
why: 'Regardless of' introduces a condition being dismissed as unimportant, and this fragment needs the main point attached. Complete the thought with what remains true no matter the result.

wrong: Having finished all my chores.
right: Having finished all my chores, I relaxed on the couch.
why: This fragment describes a completed action ('having finished') but doesn't say what happened next - it needs a main clause. Whenever you start with 'having + past participle', follow it with what you did afterward.

wrong: Which is something I've always wanted.
right: I finally got the job, which is something I've always wanted.
why: Starting with 'which' here refers to something not yet mentioned in this fragment, leaving the reader confused. Always attach this clause to the sentence describing what 'which' refers back to.

wrong: As long as you try your best.
right: As long as you try your best, that's all that matters.
why: 'As long as' introduces a condition, and this fragment needs the result or consequence attached to complete the sentence. Complete conditional phrases like this with what follows from meeting the condition.

wrong: Whether we like it or not.
right: Whether we like it or not, we have to follow the rules.
why: 'Whether...or not' introduces two possibilities, but this fragment doesn't state what remains true regardless of the choice. Complete it with the outcome that applies either way.

wrong: Until the situation improves.
right: Until the situation improves, we will continue working from home.
why: 'Until' sets up a time limit, and this fragment needs the ongoing action attached to complete the thought. Whenever you use 'until' to mark an endpoint, state what continues happening before that point.

wrong: Assuming everything goes according to plan.
right: Assuming everything goes according to plan, we should finish by Friday.
why: 'Assuming' introduces a hypothetical condition, and this fragment needs the expected outcome attached. Complete conditional phrases starting with 'assuming' by adding what you expect will happen.

wrong: For the sake of the team's success.
right: For the sake of the team's success, everyone worked overtime.
why: 'For the sake of' introduces a purpose or motivation, but this fragment doesn't say what action was taken for that purpose. Complete it with the action people took motivated by this goal.

wrong: The people who live next door.
right: The people who live next door are very friendly.
why: This fragment describes a group of people in detail but never says anything about them - the main verb is missing. Add a verb describing something about these people.

wrong: According to the latest report.
right: According to the latest report, sales have increased by 20%.
why: 'According to' introduces a source of information, and this fragment needs the actual information attached to complete the sentence. Whenever you cite a source with 'according to', follow it with what that source says.

wrong: In spite of all the challenges we faced.
right: In spite of all the challenges we faced, we completed the project.
why: 'In spite of' introduces a contrast, similar to 'despite', and this fragment needs the surprising positive outcome attached. Complete contrast phrases like this with what happened anyway, despite the difficulty mentioned.

wrong: As far as I know.
right: As far as I know, the meeting is still scheduled for tomorrow.
why: 'As far as I know' introduces a qualified statement of knowledge, and this fragment needs the actual information attached. Complete it with what you believe to be true, based on your limited knowledge.

wrong: Once you understand the basic rules.
right: Once you understand the basic rules, the game becomes much easier.
why: 'Once' introduces a time condition (meaning 'after'), and this fragment needs the resulting situation attached. Complete time clauses starting with 'once' by adding what happens after that point.

wrong: Whoever finishes first.
right: Whoever finishes first will receive a prize.
why: 'Whoever' introduces an open subject referring to an unknown person, and this fragment needs a verb telling us what happens to that person. Complete it with the action or result that applies to whoever fits the description.

wrong: Now that the exams are over.
right: Now that the exams are over, we can finally relax.
why: 'Now that' introduces a reason related to timing, and this fragment needs the resulting action or feeling attached. Complete time-related reason clauses like this with what can now happen.

wrong: Seeing how tired she looked.
right: Seeing how tired she looked, I offered to help.
why: This fragment describes an observation ('seeing how tired she looked') but doesn't say what action followed from that observation. Complete it with what you did in response to what you noticed.

wrong: She go to school every day.
right: She goes to school every day.
why: In the present simple tense, verbs must end in '-s' or '-es' whenever the subject is he, she, or it. 'She go' skips this rule. To fix it in future, ask yourself: can I replace the subject with he/she/it? If yes, the verb needs an 's' at the end.

wrong: He don't like coffee.
right: He doesn't like coffee.
why: 'Don't' is short for 'do not', which is only used with I/you/we/they. With he/she/it, you must use 'doesn't' (does not). A simple way to remember this: he/she/it always pairs with 'doesn't', never 'don't'.

wrong: The dogs barks loudly.
right: The dogs bark loudly.
why: 'Dogs' is plural, so the verb should not have an 's' - only singular subjects (he/she/it) take an '-s' verb ending. As a rule, plural subjects always take the plain form of the verb, with no extra letters added.

wrong: Everyone are happy today.
right: Everyone is happy today.
why: Words like everyone, everybody, someone, and anybody sound like they mean 'many people', but grammatically they are always treated as one single person, so they take a singular verb. Whenever you see 'every-' or 'some-' combined with 'one/body', use is/was/has, never are/were/have.

wrong: The team are winning.
right: The team is winning.
why: Words describing a group - team, family, class, committee - are treated as one single unit when they act together, so they take a singular verb. Only use the plural form if you are talking about the individual members doing different things separately.

wrong: Neither of the boys were late.
right: Neither of the boys was late.
why: 'Neither' means 'not one of the two', so even though it's followed by a plural noun (boys), the true meaning is singular. Whenever a sentence starts with 'neither of' or 'either of', use a singular verb.

wrong: The list of items are on the table.
right: The list of items is on the table.
why: The real subject here is 'list', not 'items' - the phrase 'of items' is just extra description. A helpful trick: cover up any 'of + noun' phrase with your hand, and match the verb to whatever word is left.

wrong: Each of the students have a book.
right: Each of the students has a book.
why: 'Each' always refers to one person at a time, even when it's followed by a plural noun like 'students'. Whenever a sentence begins with 'each of', treat it as singular and use has/is/was.

wrong: My family are very supportive.
right: My family is very supportive.
why: When you talk about your family as one unit that supports you together, use the singular verb. (In British English, 'family are' is sometimes used when talking about members acting individually, but for learners the safest and most common choice is singular.)

wrong: Ten dollars are too much for this.
right: Ten dollars is too much for this.
why: When an amount of money, time, weight, or distance is being discussed as a single total quantity, English treats it as one idea and uses a singular verb, even though the number looks plural. Ask yourself: am I describing one total amount? If yes, use 'is', not 'are'.

wrong: The news are very disturbing.
right: The news is very disturbing.
why: 'News' ends in 's' and looks plural, but it is actually an uncountable, singular noun in English - there's no such thing as 'one new' or 'two news'. Always pair 'news' with is/was, never are/were.

wrong: Mathematics are my favorite subject.
right: Mathematics is my favorite subject.
why: Subject names that end in '-ics', like mathematics, physics, and economics, refer to one single field of study, so they take a singular verb even though they end in 's'. Remember: the 's' at the end is just part of the word's spelling, not a plural marker here.

wrong: The number of students are increasing.
right: The number of students is increasing.
why: 'The number of' means one specific total, so the true subject is 'the number', which is singular. Compare this with 'a number of', which means 'many' and takes a plural verb - the word right after 'the' or 'a' makes all the difference.

wrong: A number of students is absent today.
right: A number of students are absent today.
why: 'A number of' means 'several' or 'many', so it behaves like a plural quantity word and needs a plural verb. Keep these two phrases straight: 'the number of' = singular, 'a number of' = plural.

wrong: Everybody have their own opinion.
right: Everybody has their own opinion.
why: Just like 'everyone', the word 'everybody' refers to a single, general person and is always grammatically singular. Pair it with 'has', not 'have', even though the meaning feels like it includes many people.

wrong: One of the reasons are cost.
right: One of the reasons is cost.
why: The phrase 'one of the...' always has 'one' as its true subject, no matter how many nouns follow it. Whenever you see 'one of the' at the start of a subject phrase, use a singular verb.

wrong: The committee have decided.
right: The committee has decided.
why: Committee, board, and jury describe a single official group making one decision together, so they take a singular verb in standard usage. Think of the group as one voice speaking, not many separate people.

wrong: Physics are a difficult subject.
right: Physics is a difficult subject.
why: Like mathematics, 'physics' names one academic subject, even though it ends in 's'. Words ending in -ics that describe a field of study almost always take a singular verb.

wrong: Neither the manager nor the staff was informed.
right: Neither the manager nor the staff were informed.
why: With 'neither...nor' and 'either...or', the verb agrees with whichever noun is closest to it - here that's 'staff', which is being used as a plural. As a rule of thumb, always look at the noun right before the verb to decide singular or plural.

wrong: The scissors is on the table.
right: The scissors are on the table.
why: Tools and clothing items made of two joined parts - scissors, glasses, trousers, pants - are always treated as plural in English, even though they refer to one object. There is no singular verb form for these words; always use 'are/were'.

wrong: Each and every student are responsible.
right: Each and every student is responsible.
why: The phrase 'each and every' emphasizes individuals one at a time, so it always takes a singular verb, just like 'each' alone does. Whenever you see 'each' anywhere before the verb, default to singular.

wrong: Ten years are a long time.
right: Ten years is a long time.
why: When a time period is described as one single stretch or duration, use a singular verb, even though the number is more than one. Ask: is this one block of time, or ten separate years? If it's one idea, use 'is'.

wrong: The police is investigating the case.
right: The police are investigating the case.
why: 'Police' is a special word in English - it has no singular form and is always treated as a plural noun, referring to police officers as a group. Always pair 'police' with are/were/have, never is/was/has.

wrong: Either of the answers are correct.
right: Either of the answers is correct.
why: Just like 'neither', the word 'either' means 'one of the two', so it stays singular no matter what noun follows it. Train your ear to hear 'either of' and 'neither of' as singular subjects.

wrong: The staff is all working overtime.
right: The staff are all working overtime.
why: When 'staff' refers to each employee doing their own separate task, rather than one unified group, English switches to a plural verb. This is different from 'staff' used as one unit - context tells you which meaning is intended.

wrong: Everyone in the class were present.
right: Everyone in the class was present.
why: No matter how long the phrase is between 'everyone' and the verb, 'everyone' itself is always singular. Don't let the words 'in the class' distract you - go back to the core subject, 'everyone'.

wrong: The jury have reached its verdict.
right: The jury has reached its verdict.
why: A jury reaching one single verdict is acting as one collective decision-making body, so it takes a singular verb. If the jury members were described as disagreeing separately, plural could be used instead - but for one shared decision, use singular.

wrong: Both of the answer is correct.
right: Both of the answers are correct.
why: 'Both' always refers to two things together, so it needs a plural noun (answers, not answer) and a plural verb (are). Whenever you use 'both', check that both the noun and the verb are plural.

wrong: There is many reasons for this.
right: There are many reasons for this.
why: In 'there is/are' sentences, the verb must match the noun that comes right after it, not the word 'there' itself. Since 'reasons' is plural, the sentence needs 'are', not 'is'.

wrong: Here's the keys you asked for.
right: Here are the keys you asked for.
why: "Here's" is a contraction of 'here is', which is singular, so it shouldn't be used before a plural noun like 'keys'. Before you shorten 'here is' or 'there is', check whether the noun after it is singular or plural.

wrong: One in five students fail the exam.
right: One in five students fails the exam.
why: In phrases like 'one in five', the actual subject is 'one', so the verb must be singular, even though 'students' appears right before the verb. Focus on the very first word of the phrase - 'one' - to decide the verb form.

wrong: The majority of people thinks this is fair.
right: The majority of people think this is fair.
why: 'The majority of' takes its verb form from the noun that follows it - here, 'people' is plural, so the verb must be plural too. When 'the majority of' is followed by a plural noun, always use a plural verb.

wrong: Economics have always interested me.
right: Economics has always interested me.
why: Like mathematics and physics, 'economics' refers to one subject area and is treated as singular, even though it ends in 's'. Don't let the final 's' fool you into thinking it needs a plural verb.

wrong: Neither answer are acceptable.
right: Neither answer is acceptable.
why: 'Neither' followed directly by a singular noun (answer, not answers) always needs a singular verb. Remember: 'neither answer is', but 'neither of the answers is' - both stay singular.

wrong: My glasses is broken.
right: My glasses are broken.
why: Like scissors and trousers, 'glasses' (meaning spectacles) is always plural in English, even when referring to a single pair. Pair these two-part object words with 'are', never 'is'.

wrong: Everyone were invited to the party.
right: Everyone was invited to the party.
why: Even in the past tense, 'everyone' remains singular, so it must be paired with 'was', not 'were'. This rule doesn't change between tenses - 'everyone' is always singular, in any tense.

wrong: The group of students was arguing among themselves.
right: The group of students were arguing among themselves.
why: Here, the sentence describes the individual students disagreeing with each other separately, not the group acting as one, so the plural verb fits better. When a sentence emphasizes individual actions within a group, plural verbs are often more natural.

wrong: Both my sister and brother is coming.
right: Both my sister and brother are coming.
why: 'Both...and' always joins two subjects into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you see the pattern 'both X and Y', automatically switch to a plural verb.

wrong: Ten miles are too far to walk.
right: Ten miles is too far to walk.
why: A distance described as one single amount takes a singular verb, similar to money and time expressions. Ask yourself: am I talking about ten separate miles, or one total distance? Here it's one idea, so use 'is'.

wrong: The audience was clapping their hands.
right: The audience were clapping their hands.
why: Since each person in the audience is clapping their own hands individually, the sentence describes separate actions, which fits better with a plural verb. This is the opposite case from a group acting as one.

wrong: None of the food were touched.
right: None of the food was touched.
why: 'Food' is an uncountable noun, so when 'none' refers to it, the verb stays singular. Check the noun after 'of' - if it's uncountable (food, water, information), use a singular verb with 'none'.

wrong: None of the students was absent.
right: None of the students were absent.
why: Unlike food, 'students' is a countable plural noun, so 'none of the students' can correctly take a plural verb. With 'none of', let the noun that follows guide whether you use singular or plural.

wrong: The dog and the cat is playing.
right: The dog and the cat are playing.
why: When two separate subjects are joined by 'and', they combine into one plural idea, so the verb must be plural. Whenever you connect two different nouns with 'and', switch the verb to its plural form.

wrong: Bread and butter are my favorite breakfast.
right: Bread and butter is my favorite breakfast.
