why: Even though 'and' usually creates a plural subject, when two foods are thought of as one combined dish (like bread and butter, or fish and chips), English treats the pair as singular. Ask: are these really two separate things, or one combined idea? If one idea, use singular.

wrong: The rich is getting richer.
right: The rich are getting richer.
why: 'The + adjective' (the rich, the poor, the elderly) is a common way to describe a whole group of people, and this pattern always takes a plural verb. Whenever 'the' is placed before an adjective to mean 'people who are ___', treat it as plural.

wrong: What she said were true.
right: What she said was true.
why: A whole clause acting as the subject of a sentence (like 'what she said') is treated as one single idea, so it takes a singular verb. When your subject is an entire clause rather than a simple noun, default to the singular verb form.

wrong: Ten percent of the students is failing.
right: Ten percent of the students are failing.
why: With percentages, the verb agrees with the noun that comes after 'of' - here, 'students' is plural, so the verb must be plural. Always check the noun following 'percent of' to decide the correct verb form.

wrong: Ten percent of the cake are gone.
right: Ten percent of the cake is gone.
why: Here, 'cake' is an uncountable noun, so even though the number 'ten percent' looks the same as before, the verb must now be singular. The rule stays consistent: percentages match whatever noun follows 'of'.

wrong: The information were useful.
right: The information was useful.
why: 'Information' is an uncountable noun in English - it has no plural form, so it always takes a singular verb. Never add 's' to 'information' and never use 'were' with it.

wrong: My advice are to wait.
right: My advice is to wait.
why: Like 'information', 'advice' is uncountable, meaning you can't count 'one advice, two advices' - so it always stays singular. Always pair 'advice' with is/was, and never add an 's' to the word itself.

wrong: I am knowing the answer.
right: I know the answer.
why: 'Know' is a state verb - it describes a mental state, not an action - so it's not normally used with '-ing'. Other state verbs to remember are: like, want, need, believe, and understand. As a rule, ask yourself: is this something you're doing, or something you simply are/feel? If it's a feeling or state, avoid '-ing'.

wrong: I have seen him yesterday.
right: I saw him yesterday.
why: The present perfect tense ('have seen') is for actions with no specific time mentioned, but 'yesterday' is a specific point in the past, so you need the simple past ('saw') instead. Whenever a sentence includes a specific past time word (yesterday, last week, in 2020), always use the simple past tense.

wrong: She is living here since 2015.
right: She has been living here since 2015.
why: The word 'since' signals an action that started in the past and is still continuing now, which requires the present perfect continuous tense ('has been living'), not the present continuous. Whenever you see 'since' or 'for' describing duration up to now, reach for 'have/has been + verb-ing'.

wrong: I am living in this city for ten years.
right: I have been living in this city for ten years.
why: Just like with 'since', the word 'for' describing a duration up to the present moment needs the present perfect continuous tense. Present continuous ('am living') only describes what's happening right now, not a duration connecting past to present.

wrong: He goed to school yesterday.
right: He went to school yesterday.
why: 'Go' is an irregular verb, meaning its past tense doesn't simply add '-ed' - you must memorize the special form 'went'. Whenever a verb feels irregular, it's worth keeping a small personal list of these forms rather than guessing.

wrong: Did you went there?
right: Did you go there?
why: After the helping verb 'did', the main verb must return to its base form, not stay in the past tense - 'did' already shows the sentence is in the past. A simple rule: 'did' + base verb, never 'did' + past-tense verb.

wrong: She didn't went to work.
right: She didn't go to work.
why: Just like with 'did', the negative form 'didn't' already carries the past meaning, so the main verb after it must stay in its base form. Never put two past-tense markers in one verb phrase - choose either 'didn't' or the past form, not both.

wrong: I am agree with you.
right: I agree with you.
why: 'Agree' is a regular action verb, not something that needs 'am/is/are' in front of it - many learners mistakenly add 'am' out of habit from other structures. Simply use 'agree' directly after the subject, with no extra helping verb.

wrong: I am understanding your point.
right: I understand your point.
why: Like 'know', 'understand' is a state verb describing a mental condition, not an ongoing action, so it doesn't take '-ing' in standard English. Whenever you notice a verb describing thinking, feeling, or possessing (understand, believe, own, belong), avoid the continuous form.

wrong: I will call you when I will arrive.
right: I will call you when I arrive.
why: In time clauses starting with 'when', 'after', 'before', or 'as soon as', English uses the present tense to describe a future event, not 'will'. Remember: only one 'will' is needed per sentence, in the main clause - the time clause stays in present tense even though it refers to the future.

wrong: If it will rain, I will stay home.
right: If it rains, I will stay home.
why: Just like time clauses, 'if' clauses describing a real future possibility use the present tense, not 'will' - this is called the First Conditional. Keep the 'will' only in the result part of the sentence, never in the 'if' part.

wrong: I am used to wake up early.
right: I am used to waking up early.
why: The phrase 'used to' has two different meanings and forms: 'used to + base verb' describes a past habit ('I used to wake up early'), while 'be used to + -ing' describes something familiar or normal now. Since this sentence uses 'am' (present), it needs the -ing form: 'used to waking up'.

wrong: I am having a car.
right: I have a car.
why: When 'have' means possession (owning something), it's a state verb and doesn't take the continuous form. 'Having' is only correct when it means 'experiencing' something, like 'I am having a great time' - possession itself should stay simple.

wrong: She has gone to market yesterday.
right: She went to the market yesterday.
why: Just like the earlier mistake with 'seen', specific past time words like 'yesterday' require the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can name exactly when something happened, use simple past, not 'has/have + past participle'.

wrong: I am not knowing what to do.
right: I don't know what to do.
why: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous ('-ing') form, even in the negative. The correct negative pattern for state verbs is simply 'don't/doesn't + base verb'.

wrong: He is having two brothers.
right: He has two brothers.
why: Describing family relationships or possessions with 'have' is a state, not an action, so no '-ing' form is used. This exact mistake is extremely common, so it's worth practicing 'has two brothers/sisters' as a fixed, memorized phrase.

wrong: I will have finished by tomorrow, I think.
right: I will have finished by tomorrow.
why: This sentence is grammatically fine, but a very common related error is dropping 'have' entirely, saying 'I will finished' - always keep the full structure 'will have + past participle' together as one unit for actions completed before a future point.

wrong: I am liking this song.
right: I like this song.
why: 'Like' is a state verb describing a feeling, not an ongoing action, so standard English avoids the continuous form (some informal marketing slogans use it for effect, but it's not grammatically standard). Stick with simple present for feelings: like, love, hate, prefer.

wrong: When I was young, I use to play outside.
right: When I was young, I used to play outside.
why: 'Used to' (with a 'd') describes a past habit that no longer happens - many learners drop the 'd' when writing because it sounds the same when spoken. Always remember the 'd' in writing: u-s-e-d to.

wrong: I have finish my homework.
right: I have finished my homework.
why: The present perfect tense needs the past participle form of the verb ('finished'), not the base form ('finish'). Always double-check that the verb after 'have/has' is in its correct past participle form.

wrong: She has wrote three books.
right: She has written three books.
why: 'Write' is irregular, and its past participle is 'written', not 'wrote' (which is the simple past form). It helps to memorize irregular verbs in three forms together: write, wrote, written.

wrong: I have did my work.
right: I have done my work.
why: The past participle of 'do' is 'done', not 'did' (which is the simple past tense form). Keep the three forms straight: do, did, done - 'done' always follows 'have/has/had'.

wrong: He has ate his lunch.
right: He has eaten his lunch.
why: The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten', not 'ate'. This follows the same pattern as 'write/wrote/written' - many irregular verbs have three completely different forms to memorize.

wrong: I was knowing him for years before we met properly.
right: I had known him for years before we met properly.
why: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous form - and because this describes something true before another past event, it needs the past perfect: 'had known'. Combine both rules here: no continuous form for state verbs, and past perfect for the earlier of two past events.

wrong: She is working here since five years.
right: She has been working here for five years.
why: Two things need fixing here: use present perfect continuous ('has been working') instead of present continuous, and use 'for' (not 'since') before a length of time - 'since' is only used with a starting point, like 'since 2018'. Remember: 'for' + duration, 'since' + starting point.

wrong: I am waiting here since morning.
right: I have been waiting here since morning.
why: As before, this needs present perfect continuous because the waiting started in the past and continues now, and 'since morning' correctly marks a starting point, matching the word 'since'. The mistake here is only in the verb tense, not the preposition.

wrong: I didn't saw him today.
right: I didn't see him today.
why: Just like with 'did', the negative 'didn't' already shows past tense, so the verb after it must be in its base form, not past tense. Watch for this exact pattern - 'didn't' + base verb - since it's one of the most frequent tense mistakes.

wrong: Yesterday, I am going to the market.
right: Yesterday, I went to the market.
why: The word 'yesterday' clearly signals the simple past tense, so mixing it with a present tense verb creates a confusing, incorrect sentence. Always match your tense to your time word - past time words need past tense verbs.

wrong: Tomorrow, I went to the market.
right: Tomorrow, I will go to the market.
why: Just as 'yesterday' needs past tense, 'tomorrow' needs future tense ('will go'), since it refers to a time that hasn't happened yet. Get in the habit of checking your time word first, then choosing the matching tense.

wrong: I have gone there last week.
right: I went there last week.
why: Once again, 'last week' is a specific past time marker, so this sentence needs the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can point to exactly when something happened, avoid using 'have/has' with it.

wrong: I am doing this since morning.
right: I have been doing this since morning.
why: The word 'since' always signals an action continuing from the past into now, which requires present perfect continuous, not present continuous alone. This is one of the most frequent patterns learners get wrong, so it's worth practicing this exact sentence type.

wrong: She said she is coming tomorrow.
right: She said she was coming tomorrow.
why: When reporting what someone said in the past ('she said'), the tense of the reported part usually shifts back one step too - present becomes past. This pattern is called 'backshifting' in reported speech, and it applies even when the original statement was about the future.

wrong: He told me he will help me.
right: He told me he would help me.
why: Just like other reported speech, 'will' shifts back to 'would' when reporting what someone said in the past. Whenever the reporting verb ('said', 'told') is in the past, shift the tense of the quoted part back by one step.

wrong: I hope you will enjoyed the party.
right: I hope you will enjoy the party.
why: After 'will', the verb must stay in its base form ('enjoy'), never in the past tense ('enjoyed'). This mistake often happens when a sentence has multiple verbs and the writer loses track of which form belongs where.

wrong: She has already went home.
right: She has already gone home.
why: The correct past participle of 'go' is 'gone', not 'went' - remember the three forms: go, went, gone. 'Went' can only be used alone as a simple past tense verb, never after 'have/has/had'.

wrong: I would went if I had time.
right: I would go if I had time.
why: After 'would', the main verb must be in its base form, not the past tense - this is the same rule as after 'will'. Keep this consistent across all modal verbs (will, would, can, could, should): always base form afterward.

wrong: If I was rich, I will buy a house.
right: If I were rich, I would buy a house.
why: This is a Second Conditional sentence describing an unreal, hypothetical present situation, which needs 'were' (not 'was') in the if-clause and 'would' (not 'will') in the result. Learn this whole pattern as a set: If + past tense (were), would + base verb.

wrong: I wish I am taller.
right: I wish I were taller.
why: After 'wish' when talking about something untrue right now, English uses the past tense form 'were' (even with 'I'), showing the wish is unreal or contrary to fact. This pattern feels unusual at first, but it's a fixed structure worth memorizing: wish + past tense, for present wishes.

wrong: I wish I will pass the exam.
right: I wish I would pass the exam.
why: After 'wish' when expressing hope about the future or frustration about someone's current behavior, use 'would', not 'will'. Combine this with the earlier rule: 'wish' pulls the following verb back into a more distant, hypothetical form.

wrong: I am here since two hours.
right: I have been here for two hours.
why: Two corrections are needed: the verb must become present perfect continuous ('have been'), and 'since' should become 'for' because two hours is a duration, not a starting point. Keep repeating this pair until it's automatic: 'for' + duration, 'since' + point in time.

wrong: I am reaching office by 9 AM daily.
right: I reach the office by 9 AM daily.
why: Since this describes a regular habit or routine, it needs the present simple tense, not the present continuous, which is reserved for actions happening right now or temporary situations. Whenever a sentence describes something that happens 'daily', 'usually', or 'every day', use present simple.

wrong: I am not agreeing with this decision.
right: I don't agree with this decision.
why: 'Agree' behaves like a regular action verb here, but it is rarely used in continuous form when expressing an opinion or stance - simple present is far more natural. Use 'don't/doesn't agree' as the standard negative form.

wrong: He was working here since 2019 before he left.
right: He had been working here since 2019 before he left.
why: Because this describes an action continuing up until another past event ('before he left'), it needs the past perfect continuous tense ('had been working'), not the past continuous. Whenever you describe duration leading up to a specific point in the past, use 'had been + verb-ing'.

wrong: I am thinking you are right.
right: I think you are right.
why: When 'think' means 'believe' or 'have an opinion', it's a state verb and should stay in simple present. ('Thinking' in the continuous form is only correct when it means actively processing an idea right now, like 'I am thinking about the problem.')

wrong: She is seeming tired today.
right: She seems tired today.
why: 'Seem' is a state verb describing how something appears, so it's not used in the continuous form. Group 'seem' together with other state verbs like appear, look, and sound when describing impressions.

wrong: I am owning a car.
right: I own a car.
why: 'Own' describes possession, making it a state verb, so it should stay in simple present, not continuous. Whenever the verb describes ownership or belonging (own, possess, belong), avoid the '-ing' form.

wrong: This bag is belonging to me.
right: This bag belongs to me.
why: 'Belong' is another state verb related to possession, and it doesn't take a continuous form. Practice this exact sentence - 'this belongs to me' - since it's a very common phrase in daily conversation.

wrong: I am preferring tea over coffee.
right: I prefer tea over coffee.
why: 'Prefer' expresses a general opinion or state of liking, so it stays in simple present rather than continuous form. This follows the same pattern as like, love, hate, and want - all describe feelings, not actions.

wrong: I like very much this song.
right: I like this song very much.
why: In English, the intensifier 'very much' goes at the END of the sentence, after the object, not squeezed between the verb and the object. A safe rule: verb + object first, then add 'very much' or 'a lot' at the end.

wrong: She has a car red.
right: She has a red car.
why: Adjectives in English always come BEFORE the noun they describe, never after it - this is opposite to many other languages, including Spanish and French. Practice the pattern: [adjective] + [noun], like 'red car', 'big house', 'happy child'.

wrong: I want to know where is he.
right: I want to know where he is.
why: Inside an embedded question (a question hidden within a longer sentence), the word order changes back to normal statement order (subject before verb), unlike a direct question. Compare: 'Where is he?' (direct question, verb first) vs 'I want to know where he is' (embedded, subject first).

wrong: He asked me what is my name.
right: He asked me what my name is.
why: Just like the previous example, embedded questions drop the question word order and go back to subject-then-verb. Whenever a question is placed inside another sentence (after 'ask', 'wonder', 'know'), keep it in statement order.

wrong: Always I go to the gym on Mondays.
right: I always go to the gym on Mondays.
why: Adverbs of frequency (always, usually, often, never) are normally placed BETWEEN the subject and the main verb, not at the very start of the sentence. The standard position is: subject + frequency adverb + verb.

wrong: I eat always breakfast at 7 AM.
right: I always eat breakfast at 7 AM.
why: The frequency adverb 'always' must come before the main verb (eat), not after it. Keep this word order fixed: subject + always/usually/often + verb + rest of sentence.

wrong: She speaks very well English.
right: She speaks English very well.
why: Adverbs describing HOW an action is done (well, quickly, carefully) usually go at the end of the sentence, after the object, not squeezed in the middle. Put the object first, then the descriptive adverb: verb + object + adverb.

wrong: He drives carefully his car.
right: He drives his car carefully.
why: Just like 'very well', the adverb 'carefully' belongs at the end, after the object 'his car'. The general rule for manner adverbs: verb + object + adverb, in that order.

wrong: I saw yesterday my friend.
right: I saw my friend yesterday.
why: Time expressions like 'yesterday', 'today', and 'last week' are usually placed at the very end of a sentence in English, not in the middle. Keep the core sentence (subject-verb-object) together first, then add the time word last.

wrong: Yesterday saw I my friend.
right: Yesterday, I saw my friend.
why: Even when a time word starts the sentence for emphasis, the subject must still come before the verb - English rarely inverts subject and verb outside of questions. Keep 'subject before verb' as your default, even with a time word up front.

wrong: I bought a nice red new car.
right: I bought a nice new red car.
why: When multiple adjectives describe one noun, English follows a specific order: opinion (nice) before age (new) before color (red). A helpful memory phrase for adjective order is 'opinion-size-age-shape-color-origin-material-purpose', often shortened as OSASCOMP.

wrong: She has long beautiful hair.
right: She has beautiful long hair.
why: Following the same adjective order rule, opinion words (beautiful) come before physical description words (long). Practice this order: how you feel about it, then its physical traits.

wrong: I have a car German small.
right: I have a small German car.
why: Size (small) comes before origin (German) in the standard adjective order. Remember the general flow: size, then where something is from, then what it's made of.

wrong: Can you tell me what time is it?
right: Can you tell me what time it is?
why: Once a question is embedded after 'tell me', the word order becomes subject ('it') before verb ('is'), just like a normal statement, instead of the inverted order used in a direct question. Practice converting 'What time is it?' into 'tell me what time it is' as a fixed pattern.

wrong: He didn't say why was he late.
right: He didn't say why he was late.
why: 'Why he was late' keeps subject-before-verb order because it's an embedded clause, not a direct question, so the inverted question order ('was he') doesn't apply here. Whenever a question is tucked inside a bigger sentence, switch it back to normal statement word order.

wrong: Only I like coffee, not tea.
right: I only like coffee, not tea.
why: The word 'only' should sit right before the word or phrase it's limiting (in this case, 'coffee'), rather than at the very start of the sentence, which changes the meaning. Placing 'only' correctly can completely change what you're actually saying, so check it carefully.

wrong: I too like pizza.
right: I like pizza too.
why: The word 'too' (meaning 'also') is placed at the END of a sentence, not right after the subject. Keep 'too' at the very end when it means 'in addition' or 'as well'.

wrong: Also I want to visit Paris.
right: I also want to visit Paris.
why: 'Also' typically goes between the subject and the main verb, similar to frequency adverbs like 'always'. The pattern is: subject + also + verb, not 'also' at the very start.

wrong: I never have been to Japan.
right: I have never been to Japan.
why: With present perfect tense, the adverb 'never' goes between 'have/has' and the past participle, not before 'have'. Keep the structure: have/has + never + past participle.

wrong: She not only sings but also she dances.
right: She not only sings but also dances.
why: In the 'not only...but also' structure, the subject should only be mentioned once, and both verbs should follow the same subject without repeating it. Keep the pattern balanced: subject + not only + verb1 + but also + verb2.

wrong: I want to eat something delicious very much.
right: I want to eat something very delicious.
why: 'Very' should be placed directly before the adjective it strengthens (delicious), not at the end of the sentence as a separate phrase. Keep 'very + adjective' together as one unit right before the noun or at the end after 'be'.

wrong: How much money do you have money?
right: How much money do you have?
why: This sentence repeats the noun 'money' unnecessarily - once you've asked 'how much money', there's no need to repeat it again in the question. Simplify by removing the second, repeated noun.

wrong: What do you want to eat food?
right: What do you want to eat?
why: Just like the previous example, 'what' already implies 'what food', so repeating 'food' afterward is redundant. Trust that your question word already carries the necessary meaning.

wrong: I can speak fluently three languages.
right: I can speak three languages fluently.
why: The adverb 'fluently' belongs after the object (three languages), following the same rule as other manner adverbs. Keep this order: verb + object + adverb of manner.

wrong: He gave to me a gift.
right: He gave me a gift.
why: When 'give' is followed directly by both a person and a thing, English drops 'to' and simply places the person first, then the thing (this is called the indirect object pattern). Only use 'to' if the person comes AFTER the thing, like 'He gave a gift to me'.

wrong: I have three sisters younger.
right: I have three younger sisters.
why: As with other adjectives, 'younger' must come before the noun it describes, not after it. Always place descriptive adjectives directly in front of the noun: younger sisters, older brother, taller friend.

wrong: She is a woman very intelligent.
right: She is a very intelligent woman.
why: The full adjective phrase 'very intelligent' must come before the noun 'woman', following the standard adjective-before-noun rule. Keep intensifiers like 'very' attached directly to the adjective, and the whole phrase before the noun.

wrong: I want a coffee cup of hot.
right: I want a cup of hot coffee.
why: The adjective 'hot' describes the coffee, so it must come directly before 'coffee', and the counting phrase 'a cup of' comes at the very front. Structure it as: [quantity phrase] + [adjective] + [noun] - a cup of hot coffee.

wrong: He speaks English good.
right: He speaks English well.
why: This is partly a word choice issue too - 'good' is an adjective (describing nouns), while 'well' is the adverb form used to describe how an action (like speaking) is done. Remember: describing a noun = good, describing a verb = well.

wrong: I want to buy shoes new.
right: I want to buy new shoes.
why: Once again, the adjective 'new' must be placed before the noun 'shoes', not after it. This is one of the most frequent word order errors for speakers of languages where adjectives typically follow the noun.

wrong: Everyday I go to office.
right: I go to the office every day.
why: 'Every day' (as two words, meaning 'daily') is usually placed at the end of a sentence, and note that 'everyday' (as one word) is actually a different word meaning 'ordinary' - a common source of confusion. Keep the time phrase 'every day' at the end of your sentence for natural flow.

wrong: I want to know how old are you.
right: I want to know how old you are.
why: This is another embedded question - once 'how old are you' is placed inside a longer sentence, it flips back to subject-before-verb order: 'how old you are'. Practice converting direct questions into embedded ones by moving the subject before the verb.

wrong: Can you tell me where does he live?
right: Can you tell me where he lives?
why: In embedded questions, not only does the word order change, but the helper verb 'does' disappears completely, and the main verb takes the 's' ending itself (lives). Remove 'do/does/did' entirely when embedding a question, and adjust the main verb accordingly.

wrong: I asked her did she like the movie.
right: I asked her if she liked the movie.
why: When embedding a yes/no question (not one starting with a question word), you need to add 'if' or 'whether', and again remove the helper verb 'did', shifting the tense onto the main verb instead. Use this pattern: asked + if/whether + subject + verb (no do/does/did).

wrong: Rarely I eat fast food.
right: I rarely eat fast food.
why: Just like 'always' and 'never', the adverb 'rarely' fits naturally between the subject and the verb, not at the very beginning of the sentence (starting with 'rarely' is possible in very formal writing, but requires special inversion which beginners should avoid). Keep frequency adverbs in their standard middle position for now.

wrong: I like it also.
right: I like it too.
why: While this isn't strictly wrong, 'too' is far more natural than 'also' at the end of a sentence in casual English - 'also' typically belongs earlier, before the main verb. Save 'also' for the middle position and use 'too' for the end position.

wrong: She sings beautifully very.
right: She sings very beautifully.
why: The intensifier 'very' must go directly before the adverb it strengthens (beautifully), not after it. Keep 'very + adverb' as a fixed unit, never split apart or reversed.

wrong: I want to tell you something important very.
right: I want to tell you something very important.
why: As with the earlier 'delicious' example, 'very' must sit right in front of the adjective (important), not trail along at the end of the sentence. Whenever you use 'very', check that it's glued directly to the word it's strengthening.

wrong: He always is late for work.
right: He is always late for work.
why: There's an important exception to the 'subject + adverb + verb' rule: when the main verb is a form of 'be' (is, am, are, was, were), the frequency adverb goes AFTER it, not before. Remember: with 'be' verbs, the order flips to subject + be + adverb.

wrong: I usually am tired after work.
right: I am usually tired after work.
why: Just like the previous example, since 'am' is a form of 'be', the adverb 'usually' comes after it, not before. Keep this exception in mind specifically for be-verbs (is, am, are, was, were).

wrong: She quickly is finishing her work.
right: She is quickly finishing her work.
why: In continuous tenses, the adverb typically sits between the helping verb (is) and the main verb (finishing), not before the helping verb. The pattern here is: subject + is/are/was/were + adverb + verb-ing.

wrong: I want to learn well English.
right: I want to learn English well.
why: The adverb 'well' belongs after the object (English), following the standard manner-adverb placement rule already covered. Keep repeating this pattern until it feels automatic: verb + object + manner adverb.

wrong: He gave carefully the documents to his boss.
right: He carefully gave the documents to his boss.
why: Manner adverbs describing the whole action, especially at the start of a verb phrase, more naturally go before the verb here, since it's not simply object-adverb - 'carefully' modifies the entire act of giving. When in doubt with more complex sentences, placing the adverb right before the main verb is often safest.

wrong: I want to visit soon my grandparents.
right: I want to visit my grandparents soon.
why: Just like other time expressions, 'soon' fits most naturally at the end of the sentence. Keep the object (grandparents) directly after the verb, and add the time word last.

wrong: Quickly she left the room.
right: She quickly left the room.
why: While starting a sentence with an adverb is possible in more advanced or literary writing, in everyday standard English the adverb usually goes between subject and verb. For clear, natural sentences, keep 'quickly' in its normal middle position.

wrong: I have already finished not my homework.
right: I haven't already finished my homework.
why: This sentence has confused word order around negation - the correct negative form combines 'have' and 'not' together as 'haven't', placed right after the subject, not scattered through the sentence. Keep negation words attached to the helping verb, not floating separately.

wrong: Not I like this idea.
right: I don't like this idea.
why: Negating a sentence in English requires the helping verb 'do/does/did' plus 'not' (don't/doesn't/didn't), placed right after the subject - simply putting 'not' before the subject is not correct standard English. Always build negatives with 'do + not + base verb', not by moving 'not' to the front.

wrong: I want to know what time does the movie start.
right: I want to know what time the movie starts.
why: This is the same embedded question pattern seen throughout this lesson - remove 'does' and shift the tense onto the main verb ('starts'), and put the subject before the verb. Practice this conversion pattern until it becomes second nature across many different questions.

wrong: I clearly can explain this to you.
right: I can clearly explain this to you.
why: With modal verbs (can, will, should), manner adverbs like 'clearly' typically go between the modal and the main verb, not before the modal itself. Keep the order: subject + modal + adverb + main verb.

wrong: In the event that you need help, call me.
right: If you need help, call me.
why: "In the event that" is a formal, wordy substitute for the simple conditional word 'if'. Replace 'in the event that' with 'if' for clearer, more natural writing.

wrong: In order to succeed, you must work hard.
right: To succeed, you must work hard.
why: "In order to" can almost always be shortened to just 'to' without losing any meaning. Whenever you write 'in order to', check if simply 'to' works just as well - it usually does.

wrong: With regard to your question, I have an answer.
right: Regarding your question, I have an answer. / About your question, I have an answer.
why: "With regard to" can be shortened to just 'regarding' or 'about', saving words without losing meaning. Choose the shorter 'regarding' or 'about' instead of the longer 'with regard to'.

wrong: In the near future, we plan to expand.
right: Soon, we plan to expand.
why: "In the near future" is a wordy way of simply saying 'soon' - the shorter word communicates the same general timeframe. Replace 'in the near future' with 'soon' for more concise writing.

wrong: Despite the fact that she was busy, she helped me.
right: Although she was busy, she helped me. / Despite being busy, she helped me.
why: "Despite the fact that" can be shortened to just 'although', or 'despite' followed directly by an -ing phrase, without the extra words. Simplify this phrase to either 'although' with a full clause, or 'despite' with an -ing phrase.

wrong: Whether or not you agree, we must proceed.
right: Whether you agree or not, we must proceed. / Regardless of your opinion, we must proceed.
why: "Whether or not" is sometimes considered slightly redundant since 'whether' alone can imply the 'or not' possibility - though this phrase is very common and widely accepted. For tighter writing, consider 'whether you agree' alone, or 'regardless' as an alternative.

wrong: Each and every one of us must contribute.
right: Each of us must contribute. / Every one of us must contribute.
why: "Each and every" combines two words with very similar meaning - choose just one for a cleaner, less repetitive sentence (this phrase is common for emphasis, but represents redundancy in careful writing). Pick either 'each' or 'every', not both together, unless deliberately using it for rhetorical emphasis.

wrong: The reason is because I was busy.
right: The reason is that I was busy. / I was busy.
why: "The reason is because" repeats the explanatory function twice - 'reason' and 'because' both introduce a cause, so combining them is redundant. Use either 'the reason is that' or simply state the reason directly, without both words together.

wrong: Absolutely essential requirements must be met.
right: Essential requirements must be met.
why: "Essential" already means absolutely necessary, so adding "absolutely" in front repeats the same emphasis unnecessarily. Trust that 'essential' alone conveys strong necessity without needing reinforcement.

wrong: Past history shows that this strategy works.
right: History shows that this strategy works.
why: "History" by definition refers to the past, so adding "past" in front is redundant. Simply say 'history' alone, since it inherently refers to past events.

wrong: Future plans include expanding the business.
right: Plans include expanding the business.
why: "Plans" already implies something for the future, so adding "future" in front is often unnecessary repetition (though sometimes used for extra clarity when contrasting with past plans). Consider whether 'future' adds real distinguishing information, or if 'plans' alone is sufficient.

wrong: Basic fundamentals of grammar are important.
right: Fundamentals of grammar are important.
why: "Fundamentals" already means the basic, essential parts of something, so adding "basic" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'fundamentals' alone, without 'basic' in front.

wrong: Final outcome of the match surprised everyone.
right: The outcome of the match surprised everyone.
why: "Outcome" already refers to the final result, so adding "final" in front is redundant. Trust that 'outcome' alone communicates the final result without needing extra emphasis.

wrong: Close proximity to the office is convenient.
right: Proximity to the office is convenient.
why: "Proximity" already means closeness, so adding "close" in front repeats the same meaning. Simply say 'proximity' alone, since it inherently describes something close by.

wrong: Unexpected surprise awaited us at the party.
right: A surprise awaited us at the party.
why: "Surprise" already implies something unexpected, so adding "unexpected" in front is redundant. Trust that 'surprise' alone conveys the unexpected nature of the event.

wrong: End result of the experiment was positive.
right: The result of the experiment was positive.
why: "Result" already refers to the end product of a process, so adding "end" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'result' alone, without the unnecessary 'end' in front.

wrong: Repeat again what you just said.
right: Repeat what you just said. / Say that again.
why: "Repeat" already means to say again, so combining it with "again" duplicates the same meaning. Choose either 'repeat' or 'say...again', not both stacked together.

wrong: Advance planning is essential for success.
right: Planning is essential for success.
why: "Planning" by nature happens in advance (before something occurs), so adding "advance" in front is often unnecessary. Consider whether 'planning' alone communicates your point just as clearly.

wrong: Free gift included with your purchase.
right: Gift included with your purchase.
why: "Gift" already implies something given without charge, so adding "free" in front repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'gift' alone conveys the concept of receiving something at no cost.

wrong: New innovation in the field of medicine.
right: Innovation in the field of medicine.
why: "Innovation" already means something new, so adding "new" in front is redundant. Simply say 'innovation' alone, without 'new' repeating the same idea.

wrong: Actual facts show the plan will fail.
right: Facts show the plan will fail.
why: "Facts" are inherently actual and true by definition, so adding "actual" in front repeats this quality unnecessarily. Trust that 'facts' alone communicates truth and accuracy without needing reinforcement.

wrong: Close scrutiny of the report revealed errors.
right: Scrutiny of the report revealed errors.
why: "Scrutiny" already implies a careful, close examination, so adding "close" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'scrutiny' alone, since it inherently means careful examination.

wrong: Empty space needs to be filled on the page.
right: Space needs to be filled on the page.
why: "Space" in this context already implies emptiness (unused area), so adding "empty" in front is often redundant. Consider whether 'space' alone communicates your meaning clearly enough.

wrong: Sudden impulse made her buy the dress.
right: An impulse made her buy the dress.
why: "Impulse" already implies a sudden urge, so adding "sudden" in front repeats the same quality. Trust that 'impulse' alone conveys the spontaneous, quick nature of the action.

wrong: Basic essentials for camping include a tent.
right: Essentials for camping include a tent.
why: "Essentials" already refers to basic, necessary items, so adding "basic" in front is redundant. Simply say 'essentials' alone, since it already implies the basic necessities.

wrong: Completely eliminate all traces of the error.
right: Eliminate all traces of the error.
why: "Eliminate" already means to completely remove something, so adding "completely" in front repeats the same emphasis. Trust that 'eliminate' alone conveys total removal without needing reinforcement.

wrong: Continue to remain calm during the emergency.
right: Remain calm during the emergency.
why: "Remain" already implies continuing in a state, so adding "continue to" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'remain calm' alone, without the redundant 'continue to'.

wrong: Final conclusion of the report is promising.
right: The conclusion of the report is promising.
why: "Conclusion" already refers to the final part of something, so adding "final" in front is redundant. Trust that 'conclusion' alone communicates the ending point without needing 'final' added.

wrong: Mutual cooperation between the two teams helped.
right: Cooperation between the two teams helped.
why: "Cooperation" already implies working together mutually, so adding "mutual" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'cooperation' alone, since it inherently involves both sides.

wrong: Postpone until later since we're not ready.
right: Postpone since we're not ready.
why: "Postpone" already means to delay until a later time, so adding "until later" repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'postpone' alone conveys the delay without needing 'until later' added.

wrong: Various different opinions were shared at the meeting.
right: Various opinions were shared at the meeting. / Different opinions were shared at the meeting.
why: "Various" and "different" both suggest multiple, distinct options, so combining them together is somewhat repetitive. Choose just one of these words, not both stacked together.

wrong: Combine together the two mixtures.
right: Combine the two mixtures.
why: "Combine" already means to bring together, so adding "together" afterward repeats the same idea. Simply say 'combine' alone, without the redundant 'together'.

wrong: Merge together the two departments.
right: Merge the two departments.
why: Just like 'combine', "merge" already implies bringing things together, making "together" afterward unnecessary repetition. Trust that 'merge' alone communicates the joining of the two departments.

wrong: Connect together these two wires.
right: Connect these two wires.
why: "Connect" already means to join together, so adding "together" afterward duplicates the same meaning. Simply say 'connect' alone, since it inherently means joining things.

wrong: Final destination of our journey is Paris.
right: The destination of our journey is Paris.
why: "Destination" already refers to the final point of a journey, so adding "final" in front repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'destination' alone communicates the endpoint without needing 'final' added.

wrong: Blend together the ingredients thoroughly.
right: Blend the ingredients thoroughly.
why: Just like 'combine' and 'merge', "blend" already means to mix together, so "together" afterward is unnecessary repetition. Simply say 'blend' alone, since it already implies mixing multiple things.

wrong: Unite together as one team.
right: Unite as one team.
why: "Unite" already means to come together as one, so adding "together" afterward repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'unite' alone conveys the joining together without needing 'together' added.

wrong: In my personal opinion, I think this is wrong.
right: In my opinion, this is wrong. / I think this is wrong.
why: "In my personal opinion" and "I think" both introduce your view, so combining them together repeats the same function unnecessarily. Choose just one way to introduce your opinion, not both stacked together in the same sentence.
